infobox.value: is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") located in both the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States") and [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") regions of the United States. Located at the geographic hub of the [heavily urbanized](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), it is bordered to the northwest, north, and northeast by [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"); on its east, southeast, and south by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"); on its west by the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"); and on its southwest by [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"). At 7,354 square miles (19,050 km2), New Jersey is the [fifth-smallest state in land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"). According to a 2024 [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") estimate, it is the [11th-most populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), with over 9.5 million residents, its highest estimated count ever. The state capital is [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), and the state's most populous city is [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). New Jersey is the only U.S. state in which every [county](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_Jersey "List of counties in New Jersey") is deemed [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") by the U.S. Census Bureau. It is the [most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density") U.S. state.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Ru-camden-campus.jpg/250px-Ru-camden-campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ru-camden-campus.jpg)

Ubiquitous [gardens](/wiki/Garden "Garden") exemplified by [landscaping](/wiki/Landscaping "Landscaping") at [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University-Camden "Rutgers University-Camden")-[Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") lend New Jersey its eponymous nickname as the Garden State.

**New Jersey** is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") located in both the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States") and [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") regions of the United States. Located at the geographic hub of the [heavily urbanized](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), it is bordered to the northwest, north, and northeast by [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"); on its east, southeast, and south by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"); on its west by the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"); and on its southwest by [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"). At 7,354 square miles (19,050 km2), New Jersey is the [fifth-smallest state in land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"). According to a 2024 [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") estimate, it is the [11th-most populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), with over 9.5 million residents, its highest estimated count ever. The state capital is [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), and the state's most populous city is [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). New Jersey is the only U.S. state in which every [county](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_Jersey "List of counties in New Jersey") is deemed [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") by the U.S. Census Bureau. It is the [most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density") U.S. state.

New Jersey was first inhabited by [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") as early as 13,000 BC. The [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") were the dominant [Indigenous group](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") when Europeans arrived in the early 17th century, and they were subdivided into dialectal groups such as the Munsee, in the north, and the Unami and the Unalachtigo, elsewhere.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") colonists founded the first European settlements in the state,[[11]](#cite_note-12) with the [British](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") later seizing control of the region and establishing the [Province of New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey"), named after the [Channel Island](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") of [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey").[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) The colony's fertile lands and relative [religious tolerance](/wiki/Toleration "Toleration") drew a large and diverse population. New Jersey was among the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") that supported the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), hosting several [pivotal battles and military commands](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_American_Revolution "New Jersey in the American Revolution") in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") earning the title, "Crossroads of the American Revolution."[[14]](#cite_note-Crossroads-15) New Jersey remained in the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and [provided](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_American_Civil_War "New Jersey in the American Civil War") troops, resources, and military leaders in support of the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"). After the war, the state emerged as a major [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") center and a leading destination for immigrants, helping drive the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") in the U.S. New Jersey was the site of many industrial, technological, and commercial [innovations](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation").[[15]](#cite_note-16) [Many prominent Americans associated with New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_people_from_New_Jersey "List of people from New Jersey") have proven influential nationally and globally, including in [academia](/wiki/Academic_institutions "Academic institutions"), social advocacy, business, entertainment, government, military, [non-profit leadership](/wiki/Non-profit_organization "Non-profit organization"), and other fields.

New Jersey's central location in the Northeast megalopolis helped fuel its rapid growth and [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") in the second half of the 20th century. Since the beginning of the 21st century, the state's economy has become highly diversified, with major sectors, including New Jersey's role as the world's largest [pharmaceutical industry](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals") hub—[[16]](#cite_note-NewJerseyPharmaCapital-17) as well as [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [finance](/wiki/Wall_Street_West "Wall Street West"), [digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media"), [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking"), and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), and it has become an [Atlantic seaboard](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") epicenter for [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics") and [distribution](/wiki/Distribution_(marketing) "Distribution (marketing)"). New Jersey is a major destination for immigrants and is home to one of the world's most [multicultural populations](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave"). Since the onset of the 21st century, re-urbanization is taking place,in New Jersey, particularly near. [New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit "New Jersey Transit") [rail hubs](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail").[[17]](#cite_note-ReUrbanizationNJ22-18)[[18]](#cite_note-ReUrbanizationNJ3-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) New Jersey is the fastest-growing state in the [U.S. Northeast](/wiki/U.S._Northeast "U.S. Northeast") and one of the fastest-growing in the United States overall, attributed to its resilient economy and aggressive [homebuilding](/wiki/Homebuilding "Homebuilding") policies.[[20]](#cite_note-NewJerseyGastestGrowingNortheastState-21)

New Jersey is one of the most developed states in the U.S. New Jersey had a [median household income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") of $99,781 as of 2023, the second-highest of any U.S. state behind [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts").[[21]](#cite_note-NJWealth-22) Almost one-tenth of all households in the state, or over 323,000, are [millionaires](/wiki/Millionaire "Millionaire"), the highest representation of millionaires among all states and the highest density per square mile.[[22]](#cite_note-Millionaires2-23) Despite not being a U.S. border state, one-quarter of New Jersey's population is [foreign born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born"), the second-highest proportion after [California](/wiki/California "California").[[23]](#cite_note-Foreign-24) The state ranks near the top on both the [American Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_American_Human_Development_Index "List of U.S. states by American Human Development Index") and the [standard Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_Human_Development_Index "List of U.S. states and territories by Human Development Index"). New Jersey is also ranked as one of the most expensive states for residents to live in.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

According to [climatology](/wiki/Climatology "Climatology") research by the U.S. [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration"), New Jersey has been the fastest-warming state by average [air temperature](/wiki/Air_temperature "Air temperature") over a 100-year period beginning in the early 20th century, which has been attributed to significant warming of the [North Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Ocean "North Atlantic Ocean").[[25]](#cite_note-NewJerseyFastestWarmingState-26)

## History

Main article: [History of New Jersey](/wiki/History_of_New_Jersey "History of New Jersey")

### Prehistoric era

See also: [Paleontology in New Jersey](/wiki/Paleontology_in_New_Jersey "Paleontology in New Jersey")

The pressure of collision between [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") gave rise to the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"). Around 18,000 years ago, the [Ice Age](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation") resulted in [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") that reached New Jersey. As glaciers retreated, they left behind [Lake Passaic](/wiki/Lake_Passaic "Lake Passaic") along with rivers, [meadows](/wiki/Meadow "Meadow"), swamps, and [gorges](/wiki/Gorge "Gorge").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Since the [6th millennium BC](/wiki/6th_millennium_BC "6th millennium BC"), [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") people have inhabited New Jersey, beginning with the [Lenape tribe](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"). **Scheyichbi** is the [Lenape](/wiki/Unami_language "Unami language") name for the land that represents present-day New Jersey.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Lenape were several [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") groups that practiced [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") agriculture in order to supplement their hunting and gathering in the region surrounding the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), the lower [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), and western [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"). The Lenape were divided into [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineality "Matrilineality") clans that were based upon common female ancestors. Clans were organized into three distinct [phratries](/wiki/Phratry "Phratry") identified by their animal sign: [Turtle](/wiki/Turtle "Turtle"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_(bird) "Turkey (bird)"), and [Wolf](/wiki/Wolf "Wolf"). They first encountered the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") in the early 17th century, and their primary relationship with the Dutch and later European settlers was through [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade").

### Colonial era

Main articles: [Colonial history of New Jersey](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_New_Jersey "Colonial history of New Jersey"), [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden"), [Province of New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey"), [East Jersey](/wiki/East_Jersey "East Jersey"), [West Jersey](/wiki/West_Jersey "West Jersey"), and [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Nieuw_Nederland_and_Nya_Sverige.svg/250px-Nieuw_Nederland_and_Nya_Sverige.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nieuw_Nederland_and_Nya_Sverige.svg)

A map of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") and [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden") in present-day New Jersey during the [colonial era](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_New_Jersey "Colonial history of New Jersey")

The [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") were the first [Europeans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") to lay claim to geographic territory in New Jersey. The Dutch colony of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") consisted of parts of the modern [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)") states. Although the European principle of [land ownership](/wiki/Land_ownership "Land ownership") was not recognized by the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") policy required its colonists to purchase land that they settled. The first to do so was [Michiel Pauw](/wiki/Michiel_Reyniersz_Pauw "Michiel Reyniersz Pauw"), who established a [patroonship](/wiki/Patroon "Patroon") called [Pavonia](/wiki/Pavonia,_New_Netherland "Pavonia, New Netherland") in 1630 along [North River](/wiki/North_River_(Hudson_River) "North River (Hudson River)"), that eventually became [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen,_New_Netherland "Bergen, New Netherland"), the first permanent settlement, local civil government and municipality in the state. [Peter Minuit](/wiki/Peter_Minuit "Peter Minuit")'s purchase of lands along the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") established the colony of [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden"), that lasted until the Dutch conquered it in 1655. Then the entire region became a territory of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") on June 24, 1664, after an English fleet under command of Colonel [Richard Nicolls](/wiki/Richard_Nicolls "Richard Nicolls") sailed into what is now [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") and took control of [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam"), annexing the entire province.

During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), the [Channel Island](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") of [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") remained loyal to the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") and gave sanctuary to the [King](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England"). In the Royal Square in [St Helier](/wiki/St_Helier "St Helier"), [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") was proclaimed King in 1649, following the execution of his father, [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"). North American lands were divided by [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), who gave his brother, the Duke of York (later [King James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England")), the region between [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") as a [proprietary colony](/wiki/Proprietary_colony "Proprietary colony") (as opposed to a [royal colony](/wiki/Royal_colony "Royal colony")). James then granted land between the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") and the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") (the land that would become New Jersey) to two friends who had remained loyal through the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"): [Sir George Carteret](/wiki/George_Carteret "George Carteret") and [Lord Berkeley of Stratton](/wiki/John_Berkeley,_1st_Baron_Berkeley_of_Stratton "John Berkeley, 1st Baron Berkeley of Stratton").[[28]](#cite_note-29) The area was named the [Province of New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey").

Since its inception, New Jersey has been characterized by [ethnic and religious diversity](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism"). New England [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists") settled alongside [Scots Presbyterians](/wiki/Scots%27_Church,_Melbourne "Scots' Church, Melbourne") and [Dutch Reformed](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed "Dutch Reformed") migrants. While the majority of residents lived in towns with individual [landholdings](/wiki/Landowner "Landowner") of 100 acres (40 ha), a few rich proprietors owned vast estates. English [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") and [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans") owned large landholdings. Unlike [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony"), [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") and other colonies, New Jersey was populated by a secondary wave of immigrants who came from other colonies instead of those who migrated directly from Europe. New Jersey remained agrarian and rural throughout the colonial era, and [commercial farming](/wiki/Commercial_agriculture "Commercial agriculture") developed sporadically. Some townships, such as [Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_New_Jersey "Burlington, New Jersey") on the Delaware River and [Perth Amboy](/wiki/Perth_Amboy "Perth Amboy"), emerged as important ports for shipping to New York City and Philadelphia. The colony's fertile lands and [tolerant religious policy](/wiki/Toleration "Toleration") drew more settlers, and New Jersey's population had increased to 120,000 by 1775.

Settlement for the first ten years of English rule took place along the [Hackensack River](/wiki/Hackensack_River "Hackensack River") and [Arthur Kill](/wiki/Arthur_Kill "Arthur Kill"). Settlers came primarily from New York and New England. On March 18, 1673, Berkeley sold his half of the colony to [Quakers](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends") in England, who settled the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") region as a Quaker colony, with [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") acting as [trustee](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee") for the lands for a time. New Jersey was governed as two distinct provinces, [East](/wiki/East_Jersey "East Jersey") and [West Jersey](/wiki/West_Jersey "West Jersey"), for 28 years between 1674 and 1702, which were part of the [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England") from 1686 to 1689.

In 1702, the two provinces were reunited under a [royal governor](/wiki/Royal_Governor_of_New_Jersey "Royal Governor of New Jersey") rather than a [proprietary one](/wiki/Proprietary_governor "Proprietary governor"). [Edward Hyde](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_3rd_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 3rd Earl of Clarendon"), titled Lord Cornbury, became the first governor of the royal colony. Britain believed that he was an ineffective and corrupt ruler, taking bribes and speculating on land. In 1708, he was recalled to England. New Jersey was then ruled by the governors of New York, but this infuriated the settlers of New Jersey, who accused these governors of favoritism to New York. [Judge Lewis Morris](/wiki/Lewis_Morris_(governor) "Lewis Morris (governor)") led the case for a separate governor, and was appointed governor by [King George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") in 1738.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Revolutionary War era

Main articles: [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Northern theater of the American Revolutionary War](/wiki/Northern_theater_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Northern theater of the American Revolutionary War"), [New Jersey in the American Revolution](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_American_Revolution "New Jersey in the American Revolution"), [Lee Resolution](/wiki/Lee_Resolution "Lee Resolution"), [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign"), [Articles of Confederation § Ratification](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation#Ratification "Articles of Confederation"), [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), [Constitutional Convention (United States)](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)"), [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), and [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg/250px-Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze,_MMA-NYC,_1851.jpg)

*[Washington Crossing the Delaware](/wiki/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_(1851_painting) "Washington Crossing the Delaware (1851 painting)")*, an 1851 portrait by [Emanuel Leutze](/wiki/Emanuel_Leutze "Emanuel Leutze") depicting [Washington's covert crossing the Delaware River](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_crossing_of_the_Delaware_River "George Washington's crossing of the Delaware River") from [Bucks County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bucks_County,_Pennsylvania "Bucks County, Pennsylvania") to [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey") on December 25, 1776, prior to the [Battle of Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Princetonwashington.jpg/250px-Princetonwashington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princetonwashington.jpg)

*Washington Rallying the Americans at the Battle of Princeton*, a portrait by [William Ranney](/wiki/William_Ranney "William Ranney") depicting [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") rallying [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") troops at the [Battle of Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton") in January 1777

New Jersey was one of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") that revolted against British rule in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). The [New Jersey Constitution of 1776](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/New_Jersey_Constitution_of_1776 "s:New Jersey Constitution of 1776") was passed July 2, 1776, just two days before the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") declared American Independence from [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). It was an act of the [Provincial Congress](/wiki/Provincial_Congress_of_New_Jersey "Provincial Congress of New Jersey"), which made itself into the [State Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature"). To reassure neutrals, it provided that the legislature would disband if New Jersey reached reconciliation with Great Britain. Among the 56 [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers "Founding Fathers") who signed the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), five were New Jersey representatives: [Richard Stockton](/wiki/Richard_Stockton_(Continental_Congressman) "Richard Stockton (Continental Congressman)"), [John Witherspoon](/wiki/John_Witherspoon "John Witherspoon"), [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson "Francis Hopkinson"), [John Hart](/wiki/John_Hart_(New_Jersey_politician) "John Hart (New Jersey politician)"), and [Abraham Clark](/wiki/Abraham_Clark "Abraham Clark").

During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), British and American armies crossed New Jersey numerous times with several pivotal battles taking place in the state. In total, there were 296 engagements that occurred within New Jersey, more clashes than occurred in any other of the 13 colonies during the war.[[14]](#cite_note-Crossroads-15) Because of this, New Jersey today is sometimes referred to as "The Crossroads of the American Revolution".[[30]](#cite_note-31) The winter quarters of the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") were established in New Jersey twice by General [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") in [Morristown](/wiki/Morristown,_New_Jersey "Morristown, New Jersey"), which has been called "The Military Capital of the American Revolution."[[31]](#cite_note-32)

On the night of December 25–26, 1776, the Continental Army under [George Washington crossed the Delaware River](/wiki/Washington%27s_crossing_of_the_Delaware_River "Washington's crossing of the Delaware River"). After the crossing, they surprised and defeated the [Hessian troops](/wiki/Hessian_(soldiers) "Hessian (soldiers)") in the [Battle of Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton"). Slightly more than a week after victory at [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), Continental Army forces gained an important victory by stopping [General Cornwallis's](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis") charges at the [Second Battle of Trenton](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Trenton "Second Battle of Trenton"). By evading Cornwallis's army, the Continental Army was able to make a surprise attack on [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey") and successfully defeated the British forces there on January 3, 1777. [Emanuel Leutze](/wiki/Emanuel_Leutze "Emanuel Leutze")'s painting of *[Washington Crossing the Delaware](/wiki/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_(1851_painting) "Washington Crossing the Delaware (1851 painting)")* became an icon of the Revolution.

Continental Army forces under Washington's command met British forces under General [Henry Clinton](/wiki/Henry_Clinton_(American_War_of_Independence) "Henry Clinton (American War of Independence)") at the [Battle of Monmouth](/wiki/Battle_of_Monmouth "Battle of Monmouth") in an indecisive engagement in June 1778. Washington's forces attempted to take the British column by surprise. When the British army attempted to flank the Americans, the Continental Army retreated in disorder. Their ranks were later reorganized and withstood British charges.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

In the summer of 1783, the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") met in [Nassau Hall](/wiki/Nassau_Hall "Nassau Hall") at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), making [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey") the nation's capital for four months. It was there that the Continental Congress learned of the signing of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), which ended the war.

On December 18, 1787, New Jersey became the third state to ratify the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution"), which was overwhelmingly popular in New Jersey since it prevented New York and Pennsylvania from charging [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") on goods imported from Europe. On November 20, 1789, New Jersey became the first in the newly formed Union to ratify the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

The 1776 [New Jersey State Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_New_Jersey "Constitution of New Jersey") gave the vote to all inhabitants who had a certain level of wealth. This included women and Black people, but not married women because they were not legally permitted to own property separately from their husbands. Both sides, in several elections, claimed that the other side had had unqualified women vote and mocked them for use of petticoat electors, whether entitled to vote or not; on the other hand, both parties passed Voting Rights Acts. In 1807, legislature passed a bill interpreting the constitution to mean universal white male [suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage"), excluding paupers; the constitution was itself an act of the legislature and not enshrined as the modern constitution.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### 19th century

Main articles: [New Jersey in the 19th century](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_19th_century "New Jersey in the 19th century") and [New Jersey in the American Civil War](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_American_Civil_War "New Jersey in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Line_of_the_Morris_Canal%2C_New_Jersey%2C_1827.jpg/250px-Line_of_the_Morris_Canal%2C_New_Jersey%2C_1827.jpg)](/wiki/File:Line_of_the_Morris_Canal,_New_Jersey,_1827.jpg)

Map of the 107-mile-long (172 km) [Morris Canal](/wiki/Morris_Canal "Morris Canal"), which crosses the state

On February 15, 1804, New Jersey became the last northern state to [abolish](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") new [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") and enacted legislation that slowly phased out existing slavery. This led to a gradual decrease of the slave population. By the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")'s end, about a dozen African Americans in New Jersey were still held in bondage.[[35]](#cite_note-36) New Jersey voters eventually ratified the constitutional amendments banning slavery and granting rights to the United States' black population.

Industrialization accelerated in the present-day [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") region of the state following completion of the [Morris Canal](/wiki/Morris_Canal "Morris Canal") in 1831. The canal allowed for [anthracite coal](/wiki/Anthracite "Anthracite") to be transported from eastern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")'s [Lehigh Valley](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley "Lehigh Valley") to North Jersey's growing industries in [Paterson](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey"), [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey").

In 1844, the second [state constitution](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Constitution "New Jersey State Constitution") was ratified and brought into effect. Counties thereby became districts for the state senate, and some realignment of boundaries (including the creation of [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey")) immediately followed. This provision was retained in the 1947 Constitution, but was overturned by the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") in 1962, by the decision *[Baker v. Carr](/wiki/Baker_v._Carr "Baker v. Carr")*. While the Governorship was stronger than under the 1776 constitution, the constitution of 1844 created many offices that were not responsible to him, or to the people, and it gave him a three-year term, but he could not succeed himself.

New Jersey was one of the few Union states (the others being [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") and [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky")) to select a candidate other than [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") twice in national elections, and sided with [Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") (1860) and [George B. McClellan](/wiki/George_B._McClellan "George B. McClellan") (1864) during their campaigns. McClellan, a native Philadelphian, had New Jersey ties and formally resided in New Jersey at the time; he later became Governor of New Jersey (1878–81). (In New Jersey, the factions of the Democratic party managed an effective coalition in 1860.) During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), the state was led first by Republican governor [Charles Smith Olden](/wiki/Charles_Smith_Olden "Charles Smith Olden"), then by Democrat [Joel Parker](/wiki/Joel_Parker_(politician) "Joel Parker (politician)"). During the course of the war, between 65,000 and 80,000 soldiers from the state enlisted in the Union army; unlike many states, including some Northern ones, no battle was fought there.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

In the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), cities like [Paterson](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey") grew and prospered. Previously, the economy had been largely agrarian, which was problematically subject to crop failures and poor soil. This caused a shift to a more [industrialized](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") economy, one based on manufactured commodities such as textiles and silk. [Inventor](/wiki/List_of_inventors "List of inventors") [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") also became an important figure of the Industrial Revolution, having been [granted 1,093 patents](/wiki/List_of_Edison_patents "List of Edison patents"), many of which for inventions he developed while working in New Jersey. Edison's facilities, first at [Menlo Park](/wiki/Menlo_Park,_New_Jersey "Menlo Park, New Jersey") and then in [West Orange](/wiki/West_Orange,_New_Jersey "West Orange, New Jersey"), are considered perhaps the first [research centers](/wiki/Research_center "Research center") in the United States. Christie Street in Menlo Park was the first thoroughfare in the world to have electric lighting. Transportation was greatly improved as [locomotion](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") and [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") were introduced to New Jersey.

[Iron](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") mining was also a leading industry during the middle to late 19th century. [Bog iron](/wiki/Bog_iron "Bog iron") pits in the [New Jersey Pine Barrens](/wiki/New_Jersey_Pine_Barrens "New Jersey Pine Barrens") were among the first sources of iron for the new nation.[[37]](#cite_note-38) Mines such as Mt. Hope, Mine Hill, and the Rockaway Valley Mines created a thriving industry. Mining generated the impetus for new towns and was one of the driving forces behind the need for the [Morris Canal](/wiki/Morris_Canal "Morris Canal"). [Zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") mines were also a major industry, especially the [Sterling Hill Mine](/wiki/Sterling_Hill_Mine "Sterling Hill Mine").

### 20th century

Main article: [New Jersey in the 20th century](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_20th_century "New Jersey in the 20th century")

New Jersey prospered through the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"). The first [Miss America Pageant](/wiki/Miss_America_Pageant "Miss America Pageant") was held in 1921 in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City "Atlantic City"); the [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel") connecting [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") opened in 1927; and the first [drive-in movie](/wiki/Drive-in_movie "Drive-in movie") was shown in 1933 in [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey"). During the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s, the state offered begging licenses to unemployed residents,[[38]](#cite_note-39) the zeppelin airship [Hindenburg](/wiki/Hindenburg_disaster "Hindenburg disaster") crashed in flames over [Lakehurst](/wiki/Lakehurst,_New_Jersey "Lakehurst, New Jersey"), and the [SS *Morro Castle*](/wiki/SS_Morro_Castle_(1930) "SS Morro Castle (1930)") beached itself near [Asbury Park](/wiki/Asbury_Park,_New_Jersey "Asbury Park, New Jersey") after going up in flames while at sea.

Through both [World Wars](/wiki/World_War "World War"), New Jersey was a center for war production, especially naval construction. The [Federal Shipbuilding and Drydock Company](/wiki/Federal_Shipbuilding_and_Drydock_Company "Federal Shipbuilding and Drydock Company") yards in Kearny and Newark and the [New York Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/New_York_Shipbuilding_Corporation "New York Shipbuilding Corporation") yard in Camden produced [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier"), battleships, cruisers, and destroyers.[[39]](#cite_note-40) New Jersey manufactured 6.8 percent of total United States military armaments produced during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), ranking fifth among the 48 states.[[40]](#cite_note-41) In addition, [Fort Dix](/wiki/Fort_Dix,_New_Jersey "Fort Dix, New Jersey") (1917) (originally called "Camp Dix"),[[41]](#cite_note-dixhist-42) [Camp Merritt](/wiki/Camp_Merritt_(New_Jersey) "Camp Merritt (New Jersey)") (1917),[[42]](#cite_note-43) and [Camp Kilmer](/wiki/Camp_Kilmer "Camp Kilmer") (1941)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] were all constructed to house and train American soldiers through both World Wars. New Jersey also became a principal location for defense in the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). Fourteen [Nike missile](/wiki/Project_Nike "Project Nike") stations were constructed for the defense of the New York City and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") areas. *[PT-109](/wiki/Motor_Torpedo_Boat_PT-109 "Motor Torpedo Boat PT-109")*, a motor torpedo boat commanded by Lt. (j.g.) [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") in World War II, was built at the Elco Boatworks in [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey"). The aircraft carrier [USS *Enterprise* (CV-6)](/wiki/USS_Enterprise_(CV-6) "USS Enterprise (CV-6)") was briefly docked at the Military Ocean Terminal in Bayonne in the 1950s before she was sent to Kearney to be scrapped.[[43]](#cite_note-44) In 1962, the world's first nuclear-powered cargo ship, the [NS Savannah](/wiki/NS_Savannah "NS Savannah"), was launched at Camden.

In 1951, the [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") opened, facilitating efficient travel by car and truck between [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") and the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), and [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") and the [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Philadelphia metropolitan area").[[44]](#cite_note-45) Subsequently, in 1957, the [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway") was completed, serving as a diagonal counterpart to the Turnpike, and opening up highway travel along New Jersey's coastal flank between [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") in the northeast and the [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey") [peninsula](/wiki/Cape_May_peninsula "Cape May peninsula") at the southeastern tip of New Jersey; in doing so, the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore") became readily accessible to millions of residents in the New York metropolitan area. In 1959, Air Defense Command deployed the [CIM-10 Bomarc](/wiki/CIM-10_Bomarc "CIM-10 Bomarc") [surface-to-air missile](/wiki/Surface-to-air_missile "Surface-to-air missile") to [McGuire Air Force Base](/wiki/McGuire_Air_Force_Base "McGuire Air Force Base"). On June 7, 1960, an [explosion](/wiki/BOMARC_Missile_Accident_Site "BOMARC Missile Accident Site") in a CIM-10 Bomarc missile fuel tank caused an accident and subsequent plutonium contamination.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In the 1960s, [race riots](/wiki/Race_riot "Race riot") erupted in many of the industrial cities of [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey"). The first race riots in New Jersey occurred in Jersey City on August 2, 1964. Several others ensued in 1967, in [Newark](/wiki/1967_Newark_riots "1967 Newark riots") and [Plainfield](/wiki/1967_Plainfield_riots "1967 Plainfield riots"). [Other riots](/wiki/King_assassination_riots "King assassination riots") followed the [assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.") in April 1968, just as in the rest of the country. A [riot](/wiki/Camden_Riot_of_1971 "Camden Riot of 1971") occurred in [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") in 1971. As a result of an order from the [New Jersey Supreme Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Supreme_Court "New Jersey Supreme Court") to fund schools equitably, the New Jersey legislature passed an income tax bill in 1976. Prior to this bill, the state had no income tax.[[46]](#cite_note-tws29decf3354-47)

### 21st century

Main article: [New Jersey in the 21st century](/wiki/New_Jersey_in_the_21st_century "New Jersey in the 21st century")

In the early part of the 2000s, two [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") systems were opened: the [Hudson–Bergen Light Rail](/wiki/Hudson%E2%80%93Bergen_Light_Rail "Hudson–Bergen Light Rail") (HBLR) in [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County "Hudson County") and the [River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(NJ_Transit) "River Line (NJ Transit)") between [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") and [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"). The intent of these projects was to encourage [transit-oriented development](/wiki/Transit-oriented_development "Transit-oriented development") in [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") and [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"), respectively. The HBLR was credited with a revitalization of [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey") and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey").[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) Urban revitalization has continued in North Jersey in the 21st century. In 2014, Jersey City's Census-estimated population was 262,146,[[51]](#cite_note-PopEst-52) with the largest population increase of any municipality in New Jersey since 2010,[[52]](#cite_note-JC2014Est-53) representing an increase of 5.9% from the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), when the city's population was enumerated at 247,597.[[53]](#cite_note-Census2010-54)[[54]](#cite_note-LWD2010-55) In 2017, Jersey City's tax base grew by US$136 million, giving Jersey City the largest municipal tax base in New Jersey.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Between [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census") and [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") Newark experienced its first population increase since the 1950s, and by [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") had rebounded to 311,549.

[Delaware Water Gap](/wiki/Delaware_Water_Gap "Delaware Water Gap") is shared between [Warren County](/wiki/Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Warren County, New Jersey") and neighboring [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Clinton_NJ_Easter_2014.jpg/250px-Clinton_NJ_Easter_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clinton_NJ_Easter_2014.jpg)

At 69.6 mi (112.0 km) in length, [Raritan River](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River") is the longest river entirely within New Jersey, flowing from [Raritan Valley](/wiki/Raritan_Valley "Raritan Valley") near [Clinton](/wiki/Clinton,_New_Jersey "Clinton, New Jersey") (above), eastward to [Raritan Bay](/wiki/Raritan_Bay "Raritan Bay") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").

[Great Falls](/wiki/Great_Falls_(Passaic_River) "Great Falls (Passaic River)") of the [Passaic River](/wiki/Passaic_River "Passaic River") in [Paterson](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey") was designated a [U.S. National Historical Park](/wiki/National_Historic_Site_(United_States) "National Historic Site (United States)") in 2009.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Belmont_Court%2C_Monroe%2C_NJ_08831.jpg/250px-Belmont_Court%2C_Monroe%2C_NJ_08831.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belmont_Court,_Monroe,_NJ_08831.jpg)

New Jersey [suburbia](/wiki/Suburbia "Suburbia") in [Monroe Township](/wiki/Monroe_Township,_Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Monroe Township, Middlesex County, New Jersey"), [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"). Despite being the [most densely populated](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"), New Jersey is mostly suburban.

New Jersey is at the center of the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), the most populated American [urban agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration"). It is bordered on the north and northeast by [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (parts of which are across the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay"), the [Kill Van Kull](/wiki/Kill_Van_Kull "Kill Van Kull"), [Newark Bay](/wiki/Newark_Bay "Newark Bay"), and the [Arthur Kill](/wiki/Arthur_Kill "Arthur Kill")); on the east by the Atlantic Ocean; on the southwest by [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") across [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"); and on the west by [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").

New Jersey is broadly divided into the [North](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey"), [Central](/wiki/Central_Jersey "Central Jersey"), and [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") geographic regions, although some residents do not consider Central Jersey a region in its own right. Across the regions are five distinct areas divided by natural geography and population concentration. Northeastern New Jersey, often referred to as the [Gateway Region](/wiki/Gateway_Region "Gateway Region"), lies closest to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in New York City, and up to a million residents commute daily into the city for work, many via public transportation.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"), along the Atlantic Coast in Central and South Jersey, has its own unique natural, residential, and cultural characteristics owing to its location by the ocean. South Jersey represents the southernmost geographical region of the [northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). The [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Philadelphia metropolitan area") includes the southwestern counties of the state, which reside within the Delaware Valley surrounding [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").

Despite the state's heavily urban character and a long history of [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), forests cover roughly 45 percent of New Jersey's land area, or approximately 2.1 million acres (8,500 km2), [ranking 31st](/wiki/Forest_cover_by_state_and_territory_in_the_United_States "Forest cover by state and territory in the United States") among the 50 U.S. states and six territories.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Northwestern New Jersey, often referred to as the [Skylands Region](/wiki/Skylands_Region "Skylands Region"), is more wooded, rural, and mountainous. The chief tree of the northern forests is the [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"). The [New Jersey Pine Barrens](/wiki/New_Jersey_Pine_Barrens "New Jersey Pine Barrens") is situated in the southern interior of New Jersey and covered extensively by mixed [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") forest; its population density is lower than most of the state.

[High Point](/wiki/High_Point_(New_Jersey) "High Point (New Jersey)") in [Montague Township](/wiki/Montague_Township,_New_Jersey "Montague Township, New Jersey"), [Sussex County](/wiki/Sussex_County,_New_Jersey "Sussex County, New Jersey") is the state's highest elevation at 1,803 feet (550 m) above sea level. The state's highest [prominence](/wiki/Prominence_(mountains) "Prominence (mountains)") is [Kitty Ann Mountain](/wiki/Kitty_Ann_Mountain "Kitty Ann Mountain") in [Morris County](/wiki/Morris_County,_New_Jersey "Morris County, New Jersey"), rising 892 feet (272 m). The [Palisades](/wiki/The_Palisades_(Hudson_River) "The Palisades (Hudson River)") are a line of steep cliffs on the west side of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") in [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") and [Hudson Counties](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey"). Major [New Jersey rivers](/wiki/List_of_New_Jersey_rivers "List of New Jersey rivers") include the [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), [Raritan](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River"), [Passaic](/wiki/Passaic_River "Passaic River"), [Hackensack](/wiki/Hackensack_River "Hackensack River"), [Rahway](/wiki/Rahway_River "Rahway River"), [Musconetcong](/wiki/Musconetcong_River "Musconetcong River"), [Mullica](/wiki/Mullica_River "Mullica River"), [Rancocas](/wiki/Rancocas_River "Rancocas River"), [Manasquan](/wiki/Manasquan_River "Manasquan River"), [Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_River "Maurice River"), and [Toms](/wiki/Toms_River "Toms River") rivers. Due to New Jersey's [peninsular](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") geography, both sunrise and sunset are visible over water from different points on the Jersey Shore.

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of New Jersey](/wiki/Climate_of_New_Jersey "Climate of New Jersey")

The state consists of two climate zones; most of the state has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (Cfa), while the northwest has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (Dfa).[[69]](#cite_note-Koppen-70) New Jersey receives between 2,400 and 2,800 hours of [sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine "Sunshine") annually.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Summers are typically hot and humid, with statewide average high temperatures of 82–87 °F (28–31 °C) and lows of 60–69 °F (16–21 °C); however, temperatures exceed 90 °F (32 °C) on average 25 days each summer, exceeding 100 °F (38 °C) in some years. Winters are usually cold, with average high temperatures of 34–43 °F (1–6 °C) and lows of 16 to 28 °F (−9 to −2 °C) for most of the state, but temperatures can, for brief periods, fall below 10 °F (−12 °C) and sometimes rise above 50 °F (10 °C). Northwestern parts of the state have significantly colder winters with sub-0 °F (−18 °C) being an almost annual occurrence. Spring and autumn may feature wide temperature variations, with lower humidity than summer. The [USDA Plant Hardiness Zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") classification ranges from 6 in the northwest of the state, to 7B near Cape May.[[71]](#cite_note-72) All-time temperature extremes recorded in New Jersey include 110 °F (43 °C) on July 10, 1936, in [Runyon](/wiki/Runyon,_New_Jersey "Runyon, New Jersey"), Middlesex County and −34 °F (−37 °C) on January 5, 1904, in [River Vale](/wiki/River_Vale,_New_Jersey "River Vale, New Jersey"), Bergen County.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Average annual precipitation ranges from 43 to 51 inches (1,100 to 1,300 mm), spread uniformly throughout the year. Average snowfall per winter season ranges from 10–15 inches (25–38 cm) in the south and near the seacoast, 15–30 inches (38–76 cm) in the northeast and central part of the state, to about 40–50 inches (1.0–1.3 m) in the northwestern highlands, but this often varies considerably from year to year. Precipitation falls on an average of 120 days a year, with 25 to 30 thunderstorms, most of which occur during the summer.

During winter and early spring, New Jersey can experience [nor'easters](/wiki/Nor%27easter "Nor'easter"), which are capable of causing [blizzards](/wiki/Blizzards "Blizzards") or flooding throughout the northeastern United States. [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") and [tropical storms](/wiki/Tropical_storm "Tropical storm"), [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"), and earthquakes are rare; the state was impacted by a hurricane in [1903](/wiki/1903_New_Jersey_hurricane "1903 New Jersey hurricane"), Tropical Storm [Floyd](/wiki/Hurricane_Floyd "Hurricane Floyd") in [1999](/wiki/1999_Atlantic_hurricane_season "1999 Atlantic hurricane season"),[[73]](#cite_note-74) and [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") in [2012](/wiki/2012_Atlantic_hurricane_season "2012 Atlantic hurricane season"), which made landfall in the state with top winds of 90 mph (145 km/h).

#### Climate change

[Climate change is affecting New Jersey](/wiki/Climate_change_in_New_Jersey "Climate change in New Jersey") faster than much of the rest of the United States. [Climatologists](/wiki/Climatologist "Climatologist") at the U.S. [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") have concluded that New Jersey has been the fastest-warming state by average air temperature over a 100-year period beginning in the early 20th century.[[25]](#cite_note-NewJerseyFastestWarmingState-26)

### Administrative divisions

See also: [List of counties in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_Jersey "List of counties in New Jersey") and [List of municipalities in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_New_Jersey "List of municipalities in New Jersey")

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") divides New Jersey's 21 counties into seven [metropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_Statistical_Areas_of_New_Jersey "Metropolitan Statistical Areas of New Jersey"), with 20 counties included in either the New York City or Philadelphia [Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Combined_Statistical_Area "Combined Statistical Area"). [Warren County](/wiki/Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Warren County, New Jersey") is part of the [Lehigh Valley](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley "Lehigh Valley") metropolitan area based in eastern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").

#### Counties

#### Municipalities

For its overall population and nation-leading population density, New Jersey is primarily [suburban](/wiki/Suburban "Suburban") and has a relative paucity of classically large cities. This [paradox](/wiki/Paradox "Paradox") is most pronounced in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"), the state's most populous county, whose 955,732 residents at the 2020 census inhabited 70 municipalities, of which the most populous is [Hackensack](/wiki/Hackensack,_New_Jersey "Hackensack, New Jersey"), with 46,030 residents. Many urban areas extend far beyond the limits of a single large city, as New Jersey municipalities tend to be geographically small; three of the four largest cities in New Jersey by population have under 20 square miles (52 km2) of land area, and eight of the top ten, including all the top five, have a land area under 30 square miles (78 km2). As of the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census")[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_Jersey&action=edit), only four municipalities had over 100,000 residents (although Edison and Woodbridge Township came very close); this number increased to seven by the 2020 census.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/New_Jersey_population_pyramid.webp/250px-New_Jersey_population_pyramid.webp.png)](/wiki/File:New_Jersey_population_pyramid.webp)

New Jersey [population pyramid](/wiki/Population_pyramid "Population pyramid")

Residents of New Jersey are most commonly referred to as New Jerseyans or, less commonly, as New Jerseyites. According to the [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the state had a population of 9,288,994, a 5.7% increase since the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), which counted 8,791,894 residents.[[7]](#cite_note-QuickFactsNJ-7) The state ranked eleventh in the country by total population and first in population density, with 1,185 residents per square mile (458 per km2). Historically, New Jersey has experienced one of the fastest growth rates in the country, with its population increasing by double digits almost every decade until 1980; growth has since slowed but remained relatively robust until recently. In 2022, the Census Bureau estimated there were 6,262 fewer residents than in 2020, a decline of 0.3% from 2020, related to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[77]](#cite_note-quickfacts-78) In 2024, the Census Bureau reported that the state population rebounded by 2.3% or 211,837 residents from April 2020. New Jersey lead the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") with a 1.3% growth rate or 121,209 residents from 2023 placing it among the top ten states nationwide for population growth.[[78]](#cite_note-Growth-79)

New Jersey is the only state where every county is deemed [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") as defined by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau").[[79]](#cite_note-80) Most residents live in the counties surrounding [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), the nation's largest city, [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), the nation's sixth-largest city, or along the eastern [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"); the extreme southern and northwestern counties are relatively less dense overall. Since the 2000 census, the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") calculated that New Jersey's [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") was located in [East Brunswick](/wiki/East_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "East Brunswick, New Jersey").[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) The state is located in the middle of the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), which has more than 50 million residents.

As of 2023, New Jersey had a [median household income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") of $99,781, the [second-highest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_income "List of U.S. states and territories by income") of any U.S. state behind [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts").[[83]](#cite_note-NJMedianIncome-84)[[84]](#cite_note-QF_table-85) Conversely, New Jersey's poverty rate of 9.4% was slightly lower than the national average of 11.4%,[[84]](#cite_note-QF_table-85) and the [sixth lowest of the fifty states, Washington, D.C., and Puerto Rico](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_poverty_rate "List of U.S. states and territories by poverty rate"). This is attributed to several factors, including the state's proximity to the major economic centers of New York City and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), its hosting the highest number of [millionaires](/wiki/Millionaire "Millionaire") both per capita and per square mile in the U.S., and the fact that it has the most [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") and [engineers](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer") per square mile in the world.[[85]](#cite_note-NewJerseyHighestScientistsEngineersWorld-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)

According to the [U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 8,752 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in New Jersey.[[88]](#cite_note-89) The top countries of origin for New Jersey's immigrants in 2018 were India, [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Filipinos_in_New_Jersey "Filipinos in New Jersey").[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### Race and ethnicity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Jersey.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Jersey.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Jersey.png)

Ethnic origins in New Jersey

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Largest_Ethnicity_in_New_Jersey_%282020_Census%29.jpg/250px-Largest_Ethnicity_in_New_Jersey_%282020_Census%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Largest_Ethnicity_in_New_Jersey_(2020_Census).jpg)

Largest ethnicity by municipality based on 2020 census

New Jersey is one of the most ethnically diverse states in the United States: as of 2022, over one-fifth (21.5%) of its residents are Hispanic or Latino, 15.3% are Black, and one-tenth are Asian. One in four New Jerseyans were born abroad and more than one million (12.1%) are not fully fluent in English. As of the 2024 census estimate,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_Jersey&action=edit) the White non-Hispanic population of the state was less than 50%, making it a [majority-minority](/wiki/Majority_minority_in_the_United_States "Majority minority in the United States") state.[[90]](#cite_note-CensusACS2024-91) Compared to the U.S. as a whole, the state is more racially and ethnically diverse and has a higher proportion of immigrants.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

  [India Square](/wiki/India_Square "India Square") in the [Marion Section](/wiki/Marion_Section "Marion Section") of [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") is home to the highest concentration of [Asian Indians](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_area "Indians in the New York City metropolitan area") in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[109]](#cite_note-Kiniry,_Laura_2006._pg._3422-111)
* [![Koreatown, Bergen County, across the George Washington Bridge from New York City](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/BroadAveColumbiaKoreatownPalisadesPk.png/120px-BroadAveColumbiaKoreatownPalisadesPk.png)](/wiki/File:BroadAveColumbiaKoreatownPalisadesPk.png "Koreatown, Bergen County, across the George Washington Bridge from New York City")

  [Koreatown, Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey#Korean_American "Bergen County, New Jersey"), across the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge") from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")

New Jersey is home to roughly half a million [undocumented immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States"),[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113) comprising an estimated 6.2% of the population, which in 2018 was the fifth-highest percentage of any U.S. state.[[112]](#cite_note-114) The municipalities of Camden, Jersey City, and Newark are considered [sanctuary cities](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city") for illegal immigrants.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

* [Five Corners](/wiki/Five_Corners,_Jersey_City "Five Corners, Jersey City"), a [Filipino](/wiki/Filipinos_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Filipinos in the New York City metropolitan region") enclave in Jersey City
* [Havana on the Hudson](/wiki/Havana_on_the_Hudson "Havana on the Hudson"), a [Cuban](/wiki/Cuban_people "Cuban people") enclave in [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey")
* [Koreatown, Fort Lee](/wiki/Koreatown,_Fort_Lee "Koreatown, Fort Lee"), a [Korean](/wiki/Korean_diaspora "Korean diaspora") enclave in southeast Bergen County
* [Koreatown, Palisades Park](/wiki/Koreatown,_Palisades_Park "Koreatown, Palisades Park"), also a Korean enclave in southeast Bergen County

* [Little India (Edison/Iselin)](/wiki/Little_India_(Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey) "Little India (Middlesex County, New Jersey)"), the largest and most diverse [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") hub in the United States
* [Little Istanbul](/wiki/Little_Istanbul "Little Istanbul"), also known as [Little Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), a Middle Eastern enclave in Paterson
* [Little Lima](/wiki/Little_Lima "Little Lima"), a [Peruvian](/wiki/Peruvian_American "Peruvian American") enclave in Paterson

New Jersey is one of the most ethnically and religiously [diverse](/wiki/Polyethnicity "Polyethnicity") states in the United States. As of 2024, a quarter of New Jerseyans (25%) were [foreign born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born"), the second most after [California](/wiki/California "California"), compared to the national average of 14.8%.[[23]](#cite_note-Foreign-24) As of 2011, 56.4% of New Jersey's children under the age of one belonged to racial or ethnic minority groups, meaning that they had at least one parent who was not non-Hispanic white.[[114]](#cite_note-116) The 2019 Vintage Year Census estimated that the state's ethnic makeup was as follows: 71.9% White alone, 15.1% Black or African American alone, 10.0% Asian alone, 0.6% American Indian and Alaska Native alone, 0.1% Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander alone, and 2.3% Two or more races. Hispanic or Latino accounted for 20.9%, while White alone (non-Hispanic or Latino) accounted for 54.6% of the population.[[77]](#cite_note-quickfacts-78) Many of the municipalities in [Bergen County, New Jersey](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"), the state's largest county, have a sizeable minority population of Hispanics and Asians.[[115]](#cite_note-117) There are also three [state-recognized tribes](/wiki/State-recognized_tribes_in_the_United_States "State-recognized tribes in the United States"), and in 2020, 51,186 identified as being Native American alone, while 96,691 did in combination with one or more other races.[[92]](#cite_note-Race_and_Ethnicity_2010-2020-93)

New Jersey hosts some of the nation's largest communities of religious and ethnic minorities in proportional or absolute terms. It has the second-largest [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_American "Jewish American") population by percentage (after New York);[[116]](#cite_note-118) the largest [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") and [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States") populations by percentage;[[117]](#cite_note-Muslims_by_state-119) the largest population of [Peruvians](/wiki/Peruvian_American "Peruvian American") in the U.S.; the largest population of [Cubans](/wiki/Cuban_Americans "Cuban Americans") outside [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"); the third-highest [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia") population by percentage; and the second-highest [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Americans_in_New_York_City "Italian Americans in New York City") population,[[118]](#cite_note-Italians-120) according to the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"). African Americans, [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") ([Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_American "Puerto Rican American") and [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_American "Dominican American")), [West Indians](/wiki/West_Indian_Americans "West Indian Americans"), [Arabs](/wiki/Arab_American "Arab American"), and [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazilian_people "Brazilian people") and [Portuguese Americans](/wiki/Portuguese_Americans "Portuguese Americans") are also high in number. New Jersey also has the fourth-largest [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_American "Filipino American") population, and fourth-largest [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American") population, per the 2010 U.S. Census.

New Jersey has the-third highest [Indian](/wiki/Indian_American#List_of_U.S._States_by_population_of_Asian_Indians "Indian American") population of any state by absolute numbers and the [highest by percentage](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Indians in the New York metropolitan area"),[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-censusbrief-124) with [India Square](/wiki/India_Square "India Square") in [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey")[[109]](#cite_note-Kiniry,_Laura_2006._pg._3422-111) hosting the highest concentration of Asian Indians in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[123]](#cite_note-Laryssa_Wirstiuk22-125) A study by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") found that in 2013, New Jersey was the only U.S. state in which immigrants born in [India](/wiki/India "India") constituted the largest [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born") nationality, representing roughly 10% of all foreign-born residents in the state.[[124]](#cite_note-IndiaNewJersey-126) [Central New Jersey](/wiki/Central_New_Jersey "Central New Jersey"), particularly [Edison](/wiki/Edison_Township,_New_Jersey "Edison Township, New Jersey") and surrounding [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"), has the highest concentration of Indians, at nearly 20% in 2020; [Little India](/wiki/Little_India_(Edison/Iselin) "Little India (Edison/Iselin)") is the largest and most diverse [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian "South Asian") cultural hub in the United States.[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130) The area includes a sprawling [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinese_in_New_York_City "Chinese in New York City") and [Koreatown](/wiki/Korean_Americans_in_New_York_City "Korean Americans in New York City") running along [New Jersey Route 27](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_27 "New Jersey Route 27").[[129]](#cite_note-131) [Monroe Township](/wiki/Monroe_Township,_Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Monroe Township, Middlesex County, New Jersey") in Middlesex County has experienced a particularly rapid growth rate in its [Indian American](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Indians in the New York City metropolitan region") population with an estimated 5,943 (13.6%) as of 2017,[[130]](#cite_note-IndiansMonroe-132) which was 23 times the 256 (0.9%) counted at the 2000 Census; [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali") is celebrated by the township as a [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") holiday. In Middlesex County, election [ballots](/wiki/Ballot "Ballot") are printed in English, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language").[[131]](#cite_note-133) [Robbinsville](/wiki/Robbinsville,_New_Jersey "Robbinsville, New Jersey"), in neighboring Mercer County, hosts [the world's largest](/wiki/Swaminarayan_Akshardham_(North_America) "Swaminarayan Akshardham (North America)") [Hindu temple](/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple") outside Asia.[[132]](#cite_note-World'sLargestHinduTempleNJ-134) [Carteret](/wiki/Carteret,_New_Jersey "Carteret, New Jersey")'s [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people") [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") community, variously estimated at upwards of 3,000, is the largest concentration of Sikhs in the state.[[133]](#cite_note-135) [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") is home to America's largest [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") community.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

New Jersey also has the third-largest [Korean](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American") population, with Bergen County home to the highest Korean concentration per capita of any U.S. county[[135]](#cite_note-Richard_Newman-137) (6.9% in 2011). It is a growing hub and home to [all of the nation's top ten municipalities by percentage of Korean population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_significant_Korean-American_populations#Top_ten_municipalities_as_ranked_by_Korean-American_percentage_of_overall_population_in_2010 "List of U.S. cities with significant Korean-American populations"),[[136]](#cite_note-BergenCountyKoreanPercentage-138) led by [Palisades Park (벼랑 공원)](/wiki/Koreatown,_Palisades_Park "Koreatown, Palisades Park"),[[137]](#cite_note-books.google.com-139) the municipality with the highest [density](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_significant_Korean-American_populations#Municipalities_with_density_of_at_least_500_Korean_Americans_per_square_mile_in_2010 "List of U.S. cities with significant Korean-American populations") of ethnic [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"). Displaying ubiquitous [Hangul (한글)](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") signage and known as the *Korean village*,[[138]](#cite_note-Palisades_Park_Municipal_Court-140) Palisades Park uniquely comprises a Korean majority (52% in 2010) of its population,[[139]](#cite_note-141)[[140]](#cite_note-142) with both the [highest Korean-American density and percentage](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_significant_Korean-American_populations#Municipalities_with_density_of_at_least_500_Korean_Americans_per_square_mile_in_2010 "List of U.S. cities with significant Korean-American populations") of any municipality in the United States.

As of 2010, 71.31% (5,830,812) of New Jersey residents age 5 and older spoke English at home as a [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"), while 14.59% (1,193,261) spoke Spanish, 1.23% (100,217) Chinese (which includes [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") and [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")), 1.06% (86,849) Italian, 1.06% (86,486) [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), 0.96% (78,627) [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), and [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") was spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by 0.89% (73,057) of the population over the age of five. In total, 28.69% (2,345,644) of New Jersey's population age 5 and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[151]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-153)

A diverse collection of languages has since evolved amongst the state's population, given that New Jersey has become [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") and is home to [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") of non-English-speaking communities:[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-155)[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)

* [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") – [Paterson](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey"), [Garfield](/wiki/Garfield,_New_Jersey "Garfield, New Jersey")
* [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") – [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey"), Paterson, [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey")
* [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") – [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey")
* [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") – Paterson
* [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") and [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") – Jersey City, all of Middlesex County, Cherry Hill, [Parsippany](/wiki/Parsippany,_New_Jersey "Parsippany, New Jersey"), [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey")
* [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") – [Gloucester City](/wiki/Gloucester_City,_New_Jersey "Gloucester City, New Jersey"), [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"), [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset_County,_New_Jersey "Somerset County, New Jersey"), and [Union](/wiki/Union_County,_New_Jersey "Union County, New Jersey") counties
* Japanese – [Edgewater](/wiki/Edgewater,_New_Jersey "Edgewater, New Jersey") and [Fort Lee](/wiki/Fort_Lee,_New_Jersey "Fort Lee, New Jersey") [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") in Bergen County
* [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") – [Bergen County (numerous municipalities)](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey#Korean_American "Bergen County, New Jersey"); [Edison](/wiki/Edison,_New_Jersey "Edison, New Jersey"); and [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey#Demographics "Cherry Hill, New Jersey")
* [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language") – Bergen County
