### Sexual orientation and gender identity

Further information: [LGBT culture in New York City](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City") and [LGBT culture in Philadelphia](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_Philadelphia "LGBT culture in Philadelphia")

New Jersey is widely regarded as an [LGBTQ+ friendly](/wiki/Gay-friendly "Gay-friendly") state and is now home to more [gay villages](/wiki/List_of_gay_villages#United_States "List of gay villages") per square mile than any other U.S. state. [Same-sex marriage in New Jersey](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_Jersey "Same-sex marriage in New Jersey") has been legally recognized since October 21, 2013, the effective date of a trial court ruling invalidating New Jersey's restriction of [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") to persons of different sexes at the time. In September 2013, Mary C. Jacobson, Assignment Judge of the [Mercer Vicinage](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey") of the [Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court"), ruled that as a result of the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States")'s June 2013 decision in *[United States v. Windsor](/wiki/United_States_v._Windsor "United States v. Windsor")*, the [Constitution of New Jersey](/wiki/Constitution_of_New_Jersey "Constitution of New Jersey") requires the state to recognize [same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[164]](#cite_note-166)

Numerous [gayborhoods](/wiki/Gayborhood "Gayborhood") have emerged in New Jersey, most prominently in [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City"),[[165]](#cite_note-NJGayVillages-167) [Asbury Park](/wiki/Asbury_Park,_New_Jersey "Asbury Park, New Jersey"), [Maplewood](/wiki/Maplewood,_New_Jersey "Maplewood, New Jersey"),[[166]](#cite_note-168) [Montclair](/wiki/Montclair,_New_Jersey "Montclair, New Jersey"), and [Lambertville](/wiki/Lambertville,_New_Jersey "Lambertville, New Jersey").[[167]](#cite_note-169) [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_capitals "List of U.S. state capitals") of New Jersey, elected [Reed Gusciora](/wiki/Reed_Gusciora "Reed Gusciora"), its first openly gay mayor, in 2018,[[168]](#cite_note-170) and Jennifer Williams, New Jersey's first openly [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") city councilmember, in 2022.[[169]](#cite_note-171) In June 2018, Maplewood, [Essex County](/wiki/Essex_County,_New_Jersey "Essex County, New Jersey") unveiled permanent [rainbow-colored crosswalks](/wiki/Rainbow_crossing "Rainbow crossing") to celebrate LGBTQ pride.[[170]](#cite_note-172) [Rahway](/wiki/Rahway,_New_Jersey "Rahway, New Jersey"), [Union County](/wiki/Union_County,_New_Jersey "Union County, New Jersey"), also unveiled its own rainbow-colored crosswalks in June 2019.[[171]](#cite_note-173) In January 2019, [New Jersey Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") [Phil Murphy](/wiki/Phil_Murphy "Phil Murphy") signed legislation mandating LGBTQ+ inclusive [educational curriculum](/wiki/Curriculum "Curriculum") in schools.[[172]](#cite_note-174) In February 2019, New Jersey began allowing a [neutral or non-binary gender](/wiki/Gender_neutrality "Gender neutrality") choice on [birth certificates](/wiki/Birth_certificate "Birth certificate").[[173]](#cite_note-175)

By number of adherents, the largest religious traditions in New Jersey, according to the 2010 [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives"), were the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") with 3,235,290; [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") with 160,666; and the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") with 138,052.[[175]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com-177) The world's [largest Hindu temple outside Asia](/wiki/List_of_largest_Hindu_temples "List of largest Hindu temples") is in [Robbinsville](/wiki/Robbinsville,_New_Jersey "Robbinsville, New Jersey"), [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey").[[132]](#cite_note-World'sLargestHinduTempleNJ-134) In September 2021, the State of New Jersey aligned with the World Hindu Council to declare October [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") Heritage Month. In January 2018, [Gurbir Grewal](/wiki/Gurbir_Grewal "Gurbir Grewal") became the first [Sikh American](/wiki/Sikh_American "Sikh American") to serve as [state attorney general](/wiki/State_attorney_general "State attorney general") in the United States.[[176]](#cite_note-GurbirGrewalNJ61stAttorneyGeneral-178) In January 2019, [Sadaf Jaffer](/wiki/Sadaf_Jaffer "Sadaf Jaffer") of [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery,_New_Jersey "Montgomery, New Jersey") became the first female [Muslim American](/wiki/Muslim_American "Muslim American") mayor, first female [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian "South Asian") mayor, and first female [Pakistani-American](/wiki/Pakistani-American "Pakistani-American") mayor in the U.S.[[177]](#cite_note-179) Large numbers of Orthodox Jews are now migrating to New Jersey from New York, due to the latter's higher cost of living.[[178]](#cite_note-NYLargestOrthodoxJewishCommunities-180) The world's largest gathering of rabbis outside of Israel occurred in [Edison](/wiki/Edison,_New_Jersey "Edison, New Jersey") on December 1, 2024.[[179]](#cite_note-LargestJewishgatheringWorldCentralNewJersey-181)

## Education

See also: [New Jersey Department of Education](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Education "New Jersey Department of Education"), [Post-secondary education in New Jersey](/wiki/Post-secondary_education_in_New_Jersey "Post-secondary education in New Jersey"), [List of high schools in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_New_Jersey "List of high schools in New Jersey"), and [List of school districts in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_New_Jersey "List of school districts in New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Old_Queens%2C_New_Brunswick%2C_NJ_-_looking_north%2C_2014.jpg/250px-Old_Queens%2C_New_Brunswick%2C_NJ_-_looking_north%2C_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Queens,_New_Brunswick,_NJ_-_looking_north,_2014.jpg)

[Old Queens](/wiki/Old_Queens "Old Queens") at [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University"), the largest state university system in New Jersey

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Cleveland_Tower%2C_Princeton_University_Graduate_College%2C_NJ_-_pinnacles.jpg/250px-Cleveland_Tower%2C_Princeton_University_Graduate_College%2C_NJ_-_pinnacles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleveland_Tower,_Princeton_University_Graduate_College,_NJ_-_pinnacles.jpg)

[Cleveland Tower](/wiki/Cleveland_Tower "Cleveland Tower") at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), an [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") university and one of the world's most prominent research institutions, served briefly as the U.S. Capitol in the 18th century.

As of the 2020–2021 school year, there were 686 operating [districts in the state](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_New_Jersey "List of school districts in New Jersey"). Of these, 599 were [traditional public school](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_States "Education in the United States") districts and 87 were [charter school](/wiki/Charter_schools_in_the_United_States "Charter schools in the United States") districts.[[182]](#cite_note-184)[[183]](#cite_note-185) The [NJDOE](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Education "New Jersey Department of Education") reported a total district enrollment of 1,362,400 students, the lowest total enrollment since the early 2000s, though these figures do not consider [homeschooled](/wiki/Homeschooling "Homeschooling") students or those attending [out-of-state schools](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school").[[184]](#cite_note-186) New Jersey public schools emphasize [STEM](/wiki/STEM "STEM") subjects, and New Jersey is home to more scientists and engineers per square mile than anywhere else in the world.[[85]](#cite_note-NewJerseyHighestScientistsEngineersWorld-86)[[185]](#cite_note-NewJerseyHighestDensityScientistsEngineers-187)

### Educational standards

New Jersey is known for the high quality of its education. In 2024, New Jersey spent the second-most per public school student among all states, behind [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), amounting to $26,600 spent per pupil.[[186]](#cite_note-NJHighEducationExpenditurePerPupil-188) In 2018, over 50% of the expenditure was allocated to student instruction.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

New Jersey is home to several prestigious private universities, including [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") in [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey") in [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey"), one of the world's most prominent [research universities](/wiki/Research_university "Research university"), which was ranked first for the tenth consecutive year among all national universities by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* in 2025 and has ranked first in comparable lists published by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* and *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*,[[188]](#cite_note-PrincetonTopUniversityUSNews-190)[[189]](#cite_note-ForbesPrincetonTopUniversity-191)[[190]](#cite_note-WSJPrincetonTopUniversity-192) and public universities such as [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University"), headquartered in [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey"), [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"), the state's flagship institution of higher education.[[191]](#cite_note-RutgersRef-193)

In 2019, 2020, and 2021, *Education Week* also ranked New Jersey public schools the best of all U.S. states.[[192]](#cite_note-194)[[193]](#cite_note-KellyHeyboer20202-195)

In 2016, nine New Jersey-based [high schools](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_the_United_States "Secondary education in the United States") were ranked among the top 25 in the nation, more than any other state, in "America's Top High Schools 2016", published by *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*.[[194]](#cite_note-196)

In November 2023, New Jersey Governor [Phil Murphy](/wiki/Phil_Murphy "Phil Murphy") signed into law legislation eliminating testing for prospective teachers in reading, writing, and math, replacing it with an alternative certification process.[[195]](#cite_note-197)

## Economy

See also: [New Jersey locations by per capita income](/wiki/New_Jersey_locations_by_per_capita_income "New Jersey locations by per capita income")

The [U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/U.S._Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis") estimates that New Jersey's [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") in the third quarter of 2022 was $753 billion.[[196]](#cite_note-198)

### Affluence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Map_of_Income_by_Location_in_New_Jersey.png/250px-Map_of_Income_by_Location_in_New_Jersey.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Income_by_Location_in_New_Jersey.png)

A heat map showing median income distribution in New Jersey by county

New Jersey's [per capita gross state product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_GDP_per_capita_(nominal) "List of U.S. states by GDP per capita (nominal)") routinely ranks among the highest in the nation. In 2020, New Jersey had more [millionaires](/wiki/Millionaire "Millionaire") both per capita and per square mile, representing roughly 9.76% of all New Jersey household, than any state in the nation.[[22]](#cite_note-Millionaires2-23)

The state is ranked second in the nation by the number of places with per capita incomes exceeding the national average with 76.4%. Three of New Jersey's counties are among the 20 highest income U.S. counties in the nation as of 2023.[[197]](#cite_note-199)

As of May 2025, the state's [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") rate was 4.8%.[[198]](#cite_note-200)

### Fiscal policy

New Jersey has seven [tax brackets](/wiki/Tax_bracket "Tax bracket") that determine [state income tax](/wiki/State_income_tax "State income tax") rates, which range from 1.4% (for income below $20,000) to 10.75% (for income above $1,000,000).[[199]](#cite_note-201)

The standard [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") rate as of January 1, 2018, is 6.625%, applicable to all retail sales unless specifically exempt by law. This rate, which is comparably lower than that of New York City, often attracts numerous shoppers from New York City, often to suburban [Paramus](/wiki/Paramus "Paramus"), New Jersey, which has five malls, one of which (the [Garden State Plaza](/wiki/Garden_State_Plaza "Garden State Plaza")) has over 2 million square feet (200,000 m2) of retail space. [Tax exemptions](/wiki/Tax_exemption "Tax exemption") include most food items for at-home preparation, medications, most clothing, footwear and disposable paper products for use in the home.[[200]](#cite_note-202) There are 27 [Urban Enterprise Zone](/wiki/Urban_Enterprise_Zone "Urban Enterprise Zone") statewide, including sections of [Paterson](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey"), [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_New_Jersey "Elizabeth, New Jersey"), and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"). In addition to other benefits to encourage employment within the zone, shoppers can take advantage of a reduced 3.3125% [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") rate (half the rate charged statewide) at eligible merchants.[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-205)

New Jersey has the highest cumulative tax rate of all 50 states with residents paying a total of $68 billion in state and local taxes annually with a per capita burden of $7,816 at a rate of 12.9% of income.[[204]](#cite_note-206) All [real property](/wiki/Real_property "Real property") located in the state is subject to [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") unless specifically exempted by statute. New Jersey does not assess an intangible personal property tax or an [estate tax](/wiki/Estate_tax_in_the_United_States "Estate tax in the United States"), but it does impose an [inheritance tax](/wiki/Inheritance_tax "Inheritance tax") (which is levied only on heirs who are not direct descendants).[[205]](#cite_note-207) In 2023, Governor [Phil Murphy](/wiki/Phil_Murphy "Phil Murphy") signed into law a new tax-relief program known as StayNJ that will provide for an annual property-tax cut of 50% for those aged 65 and over with incomes below $500,000; the cut will go into effect in January 2026 and be capped at $6,500, but with this cap rising as indexed to the increase in New Jersey's overall property taxes.[[206]](#cite_note-StayNJ50%PropertyTaxCutSeniorCitizens-208)[[207]](#cite_note-209)

### Federal taxation disparity

New Jersey consistently ranks as having one of the highest proportional levels of disparity of any state in the U.S., based upon what it receives from the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") relative to what it pays. In 2015, [WalletHub](/wiki/WalletHub "WalletHub") ranked New Jersey the state that was least dependent on federal government aid overall and the state with the fourth-lowest return on taxpayer investment from the federal government, at 48 cents per dollar.[[208]](#cite_note-210) In 2023, New Jersey sent approximately $183 billion to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") in taxes but only received approximately $113 billion in federal investment resulting in each state resident sending the federal government approximately $7,500 more than they get back. In 2025, WalletHub again ranked New Jersey as the least dependent on federal government aid overall with now the second-lowest return on taxpayer spending from the federal government, at 48 cents per dollar.[[209]](#cite_note-211)

New Jersey has one of the highest tax burdens of any state the nation.[[210]](#cite_note-taxfoundation-212) Factors for this include the large federal tax liability, which is not adjusted for New Jersey's higher cost of living and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") funding formulas.

### Industries

Further information: [Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in New Jersey](/wiki/Biotech_and_pharmaceutical_companies_in_New_Jersey "Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Cranberrys_beim_Ernten.jpeg/250px-Cranberrys_beim_Ernten.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Cranberrys_beim_Ernten.jpeg)

[Cranberry](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry") harvest

New Jersey's economy is multifaceted, featuring high levels of both [productivity](/wiki/Productivity "Productivity") and [retail consumption](/wiki/Retail "Retail"); the Garden State's economy comprises the [pharmaceutical industry](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), the [financial industry](/wiki/Financial_industry "Financial industry"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking"), [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), [gambling](/wiki/Gambling "Gambling"), food processing, electrical equipment manufacturing, printing, and publishing. New Jersey's agricultural outputs are nursery stock, horses, vegetables, fruits and nuts, seafood, and dairy products.[[211]](#cite_note-213) New Jersey ranks second among states in [blueberry](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry") production, third in [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry") and [spinach](/wiki/Spinach "Spinach"), and fourth in [bell peppers](/wiki/Bell_peppers "Bell peppers"), [peaches](/wiki/Peach "Peach"), and [head lettuce](/wiki/Lettuce "Lettuce").[[212]](#cite_note-214) The state harvests the fourth-largest number of acres planted with [asparagus](/wiki/Asparagus "Asparagus").[[213]](#cite_note-215) [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") has become an [East Coast epicenter](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") for [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics") and [warehouse](/wiki/Warehouse "Warehouse") construction.[[214]](#cite_note-SouthJerseyEastCoastLogisticsEpicenter-216) In 2025, 99.7% of businesses in New Jersey were [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business"), employing 48.8% of the state's workforce.[[215]](#cite_note-217)

#### Scientific economy

New Jersey has a strong [scientific economy](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") and is home to major pharmaceutical and telecommunications firms, drawing on the state's large and well-educated labor pool, including one of the highest concentrations of [engineers](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer") and other scientists in the world. There is also a robust service economy in retail sales, education, and real estate, serving residents who work in New York City or Philadelphia. [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") invented the first [electric light bulb](/wiki/Electric_light_bulb "Electric light bulb") at his home in [Menlo Park, Edison](/wiki/Menlo_Park,_New_Jersey "Menlo Park, New Jersey") in 1879. New Jersey is also a key participant in the [renewable wind industry](/wiki/Wind_industry "Wind industry"). New Jersey has more scientists and engineers per square mile than anywhere in the world,[[216]](#cite_note-218) and is a global leader in [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences"), and [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology").[[217]](#cite_note-219)[[218]](#cite_note-220)

#### Corporate and retail

New Jersey hosts numerous business headquarters, including twenty-four [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies.[[219]](#cite_note-221) [Paramus](/wiki/Paramus,_New_Jersey "Paramus, New Jersey") in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") has become the top retail [ZIP code](/wiki/ZIP_code "ZIP code") (07652) in the United States, with the municipality generating over US$6 billion in annual retail sales.[[220]](#cite_note-ParamusNumberOneRetailZipCode-222) Several New Jersey counties, including [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset_County,_New_Jersey "Somerset County, New Jersey") (7), [Morris](/wiki/Morris_County,_New_Jersey "Morris County, New Jersey") (10), [Hunterdon](/wiki/Hunterdon_County,_New_Jersey "Hunterdon County, New Jersey") (13), Bergen (21), and [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth_County,_New_Jersey "Monmouth County, New Jersey") (42), have been ranked among the [highest-income counties in the United States](/wiki/Highest-income_counties_in_the_United_States "Highest-income counties in the United States").

#### Shipping, manufacturing, and logistics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/PortNewarkNJTurnpike.jpg/250px-PortNewarkNJTurnpike.jpg)](/wiki/File:PortNewarkNJTurnpike.jpg)

The [Port Newark-Elizabeth Marine Terminal](/wiki/Port_Newark-Elizabeth_Marine_Terminal "Port Newark-Elizabeth Marine Terminal") was the world's first [container seaport](/wiki/Container_port "Container port") and remains one of the world's largest and busiest.

Shipping is a key industry in New Jersey because of the state's strategic geographic location, the [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey") being the busiest [seaport](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") on the [U.S. East Coast](/wiki/U.S._East_Coast "U.S. East Coast"). The [Port Newark-Elizabeth Marine Terminal](/wiki/Port_Newark-Elizabeth_Marine_Terminal "Port Newark-Elizabeth Marine Terminal") was the world's first [container port](/wiki/Container_port "Container port") and today is one of the world's largest and busiest. New Jersey's location at the center of the [Eastern North American](/wiki/Eastern_North_America "Eastern North America") population belt has made the state a prime hub for the logistics, warehousing, and [supply chain](/wiki/Supply_chain "Supply chain") management industries. The [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") economy in New Jersey had declined for several decades in the post-[Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") era but has since resumed growth.

#### Tourism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/A_sunset_view_of_the_beach_in_Atlantic_City%2C_NJ.jpg/250px-A_sunset_view_of_the_beach_in_Atlantic_City%2C_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_sunset_view_of_the_beach_in_Atlantic_City,_NJ.jpg)

[Sunset](/wiki/Sunset "Sunset") on the beach at [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"), an [oceanfront resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") and the nexus of New Jersey's [gambling industry](/wiki/Casino "Casino")

New Jersey's location at the center of the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") and its extensive transportation system have put over one-third of all United States residents and many Canadian residents within overnight distance by land. This accessibility to consumer revenue has enabled [seaside resorts](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") such as [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey") and the remainder of the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"), as well as the state's other natural and cultural attractions, to contribute significantly to the record 111 million tourist visits to New Jersey in 2018, providing US$44.7 billion in tourism revenue, directly supporting 333,860 jobs, sustaining more than 531,000 jobs overall including peripheral impacts, and generating US$5 billion in state and local tax revenue.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

#### Gambling

Main article: [Gambling in New Jersey](/wiki/Gambling_in_New_Jersey "Gambling in New Jersey")

In 1976, a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") by Jersey voters approved [casino](/wiki/Casino "Casino") gambling in Atlantic City, where the first legalized casino opened in 1978.[[222]](#cite_note-224) At that time, [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Valley "Las Vegas Valley") was the only other casino resort in the country.[[223]](#cite_note-225) Today, several casinos lie along the [Atlantic City Boardwalk](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk "Atlantic City Boardwalk"), the oldest and longest boardwalk in the world, at 5+1⁄2 miles (8.9 km) in length.[[224]](#cite_note-Oyster2015-226) Atlantic City experienced a dramatic contraction in its stature as a gambling destination after 2010, including the closure of multiple casinos since 2014, spurred by competition from the advent of legalized gambling in other northeastern U.S. states.[[225]](#cite_note-227)[[226]](#cite_note-228)

On February 26, 2013, Governor Chris Christie signed [online gambling](/wiki/Online_gambling "Online gambling") into law.[[227]](#cite_note-229) [Sports betting](/wiki/Sports_betting "Sports betting") has become a growing source of gambling revenue in New Jersey, with sportsbooks bringing in almost $12 billion in bets, making over $1 billion in revenue in 2023. Since being legalized across the nation by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") on May 14, 2018, New Jersey led all states in sports betting handle until New York passed them.[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231) In September 2022, the lifetime revenue from [online casinos](/wiki/Online_casinos "Online casinos") operating in New Jersey for the nine years since the industry's launch had surpassed $5 billion.[[230]](#cite_note-232)

#### Media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/USA_telephone_area_code_map_-_New_Jersey.svg/250px-USA_telephone_area_code_map_-_New_Jersey.svg.png)](/wiki/File:USA_telephone_area_code_map_-_New_Jersey.svg)

New Jersey's telephone [area codes](/wiki/Telephone_numbering_plan "Telephone numbering plan")

##### Television and film production

Main article: [Television and film of New Jersey](/wiki/Television_and_film_of_New_Jersey "Television and film of New Jersey")

New Jersey is a growing center for [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") and [television production](/wiki/Television_production "Television production"),[[231]](#cite_note-NewJerseyFilmTelevsionProductionHub1-233) with [media companies](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media"), enticed by its proximity to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), in conjunction with [tax incentives](/wiki/Tax_incentive "Tax incentive"), collectively spending billions of dollars to develop large new [studio facilities](/wiki/Film_studio "Film studio") and [sound stage](/wiki/Sound_stage "Sound stage") complexes.[[232]](#cite_note-NewJerseyFilmTelevisionProductionHub2-234) Motion picture technology was developed by [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison"), with much of his early work done at his [West Orange](/wiki/West_Orange,_New_Jersey "West Orange, New Jersey") laboratory. [Edison's Black Maria](/wiki/Edison%27s_Black_Maria "Edison's Black Maria") was the first motion picture studio. [America's first motion picture industry](/wiki/America%27s_first_motion_picture_industry "America's first motion picture industry") started in 1907 in [Fort Lee](/wiki/Fort_Lee,_New_Jersey "Fort Lee, New Jersey") and the first studio was constructed there in 1909.[[233]](#cite_note-235) [DuMont Laboratories](/wiki/DuMont_Laboratories "DuMont Laboratories") in [Passaic](/wiki/Passaic,_New_Jersey "Passaic, New Jersey") developed early sets and made the first broadcast to the private home.

A number of television shows and films have been filmed in New Jersey. Since 1978, the state has maintained a Motion Picture and Television Commission to encourage filming in-state.[[234]](#cite_note-236) New Jersey has long offered tax credits to television producers. Governor Chris Christie suspended the credits in 2010, but the [New Jersey State Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Legislature "New Jersey State Legislature") in 2011 approved the restoration and expansion of the tax credit program. Under bills passed by both the state Senate and Assembly, the program offers 20 percent tax credits (22% in [urban enterprise zones](/wiki/Urban_enterprise_zone "Urban enterprise zone")) to television and film productions that shoot in the state and meet set standards for hiring and local spending.[[235]](#cite_note-crewsback-237) When Governor Phil Murphy took office, he instated the New Jersey Film & Digital Media Tax Credit Program in 2018 and expanded it in 2020. The benefits include a 30% tax credit on film projects and a 40% subsidy for studio developments.[[236]](#cite_note-238)

##### Television stations

New Jersey has several [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") member stations: [WNET](/wiki/WNET "WNET") (13) in [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), [WNJN](/wiki/WNJN_(TV) "WNJN (TV)") (50) in [Montclair](/wiki/Montclair,_New_Jersey "Montclair, New Jersey"), WNJB (58) in [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_NJ "New Brunswick, NJ"), WNJS (23) in [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") and WNJT (52) in [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_NJ "Trenton, NJ").

There are no standard commercial [network affiliates](/wiki/Network_affiliate "Network affiliate") in the state. [WMGM-TV](/wiki/WMGM-TV "WMGM-TV") ([Wildwood](/wiki/Wildwood,_New_Jersey "Wildwood, New Jersey")) lost its affiliation with [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") in 2014. Viewers in northern New Jersey receive [New York City market](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City#Television "Media in New York City") stations over cable or over the air; southern New Jersey viewers receive [Philadelphia market](/wiki/Media_in_Philadelphia#Television_stations "Media in Philadelphia") stations over cable or over the air.

WMGM now affiliates with the [True Crime Network](/wiki/True_Crime_Network "True Crime Network"). [WJLP](/wiki/WJLP "WJLP") ([Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_New_Jersey "Middletown, New Jersey")) affiliates with the retro network [MeTV](/wiki/MeTV "MeTV"). There are [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") affiliates in Fort Lee, Linden and Mount Laurel, and [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") affiliates in Paterson and Vineland.

#### Finance as Wall Street West

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Jersey_City_Skyline_sunset_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jersey_City_Skyline_sunset_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jersey_City_Skyline_sunset_(cropped).jpg)

The developing waterfront skyline of [Downtown Jersey City](/wiki/Downtown_Jersey_City "Downtown Jersey City"), dubbed [Wall Street West](/wiki/Wall_Street_West "Wall Street West")[[240]](#cite_note-JerseyCityWaterfrontWallStreetWest-242)

[Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City")'s [Hudson River waterfront](/wiki/Downtown_Jersey_City "Downtown Jersey City"), from [Exchange Place](/wiki/Exchange_Place_(Jersey_City) "Exchange Place (Jersey City)") to [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Jersey_City "Newport, Jersey City"), is known as [Wall Street West](/wiki/Wall_Street_West "Wall Street West")[[240]](#cite_note-JerseyCityWaterfrontWallStreetWest-242) and has over 13 million square feet of [Class A office space](/wiki/Class_A_office "Class A office"). One third of the [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") jobs in the city are in the [financial services](/wiki/Finance "Finance") sector: more than 60% are in the [securities industry](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)"), 20% are in banking and 8% in [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance").[[241]](#cite_note-243) Jersey City is home to the headquarters of [Verisk Analytics](/wiki/Verisk_Analytics "Verisk Analytics") and [Lord Abbett](/wiki/Lord_Abbett "Lord Abbett"),[[242]](#cite_note-StarLedger-244) a privately held money management firm.[[243]](#cite_note-245) Companies such as [Computershare](/wiki/Computershare "Computershare"), [ADP](/wiki/Automatic_Data_Processing "Automatic Data Processing"), [IPC Systems](/wiki/IPC_Systems "IPC Systems"), and [Fidelity Investments](/wiki/Fidelity_Investments "Fidelity Investments") also conduct operations in the city.[[244]](#cite_note-246) In 2014, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* magazine moved its headquarters to the district, having been awarded a $27 million tax grant in exchange for bringing 350 jobs to the city over a ten-year period.[[245]](#cite_note-247) By the early 2020s, the construction of residential [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper") Downtown made median rental rates in Jersey City amongst the highest of any city in the United States.[[246]](#cite_note-248)

#### Natural resources and energy

See also: [List of power stations in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_New_Jersey "List of power stations in New Jersey")

Limited mining activity of [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), iron, and [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese") still takes place in the area in and around the [Franklin Furnace](/wiki/Franklin_Furnace "Franklin Furnace") in [Sussex County](/wiki/Sussex_County,_New_Jersey "Sussex County, New Jersey").

Although New Jersey is home to many energy-intensive industries, its energy consumption is only 2.7% of the U.S. total, and its [carbon dioxide emissions](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide_emissions "Carbon dioxide emissions") are 0.8% of the U.S. total. New Jersey's electricity comes primarily from natural gas and nuclear power.[[247]](#cite_note-249) New Jersey is seventh in the nation in [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power_in_New_Jersey "Solar power in New Jersey") installations,[[248]](#cite_note-250) enabled by one of the country's most favorable [net metering](/wiki/Net_metering "Net metering") policies and [renewable portfolio standard](/wiki/Renewable_portfolio_standards_in_the_United_States "Renewable portfolio standards in the United States"). The state has more than 140,000 solar installations.[[249]](#cite_note-251)

## Environment

Due to past industrial activity, New Jersey has more [Superfund](/wiki/Superfund "Superfund") toxic waste sites than any other state in the union despite its small geographic size. By 2024, only 35 of New Jersey's Superfund sites (out of about 150 that have been on the EPA's list since the Superfund law was passed in 1980) have been cleaned up.[[250]](#cite_note-NPL2024-252)

In late 2023, a concern became public about [PFAs](/wiki/Per-_and_polyfluoroalkyl_substances "Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances") (so-called "forever chemicals") existing in the state's water supplies.[[251]](#cite_note-PFAS-253)

## Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in New Jersey](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_Jersey "Transportation in New Jersey")

New Jersey's population density and location at the geographic center of the [Northeast Megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_Megalopolis "Northeast Megalopolis") have rendered it a vital transportation for hub for both passengers and industry.

### Roadways

See also: [State highways in New Jersey](/wiki/State_highways_in_New_Jersey "State highways in New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Map_of_New_Jersey_NA.png/250px-Map_of_New_Jersey_NA.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_New_Jersey_NA.png)

Map of New Jersey's major transportation networks and cities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/George_Washington_Bridge_from_New_Jersey-edit.jpg/250px-George_Washington_Bridge_from_New_Jersey-edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Washington_Bridge_from_New_Jersey-edit.jpg)

The [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge"), connecting [Fort Lee](/wiki/Fort_Lee,_New_Jersey "Fort Lee, New Jersey") (foreground) in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") across the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (background), is the world's busiest motor vehicle bridge.[[252]](#cite_note-panynj.gov-254)[[253]](#cite_note-abcgwb-255)

The [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") is one of the most prominent and heavily trafficked roadways in the United States. This [toll road](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road"), which overlaps with [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_New_Jersey "Interstate 95 in New Jersey") for much of its length, carries traffic between [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") and New York, and up and down the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") in general. Commonly referred to as simply "the Turnpike", it is known for its numerous [rest areas](/wiki/Rest_area "Rest area") named after prominent New Jerseyans.

The [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway"), or simply "the Parkway", carries relatively more in-state traffic than interstate traffic and runs from New Jersey's northern border to its southernmost tip at [Cape May](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey"). It is the main route that connects the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") to the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"). With a total of fifteen travel and six shoulder lanes, the [Driscoll Bridge](/wiki/Driscoll_Bridge "Driscoll Bridge") on the Parkway, spanning the [Raritan River](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River") in [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"), is the widest motor vehicle bridge in the world by number of lanes as well as one of the busiest.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

New Jersey is connected to New York City via various key bridges and tunnels. The double-decked [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge") carries the heaviest load of motor vehicle traffic of any bridge in the world, at 102 million vehicles per year, across fourteen lanes.[[252]](#cite_note-panynj.gov-254)[[253]](#cite_note-abcgwb-255) It connects [Fort Lee, New Jersey](/wiki/Fort_Lee,_New_Jersey "Fort Lee, New Jersey") to the [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan") neighborhood of [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"), and carries [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95 "Interstate 95") and [U.S. Route 1/9](/wiki/U.S._Route_1/9 "U.S. Route 1/9") across the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). The [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel") connects to [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") carrying [New Jersey Route 495](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_495 "New Jersey Route 495"), and the [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel") connects to [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") carrying [Interstate 78](/wiki/Interstate_78_in_New_Jersey "Interstate 78 in New Jersey"). New Jersey is also connected to [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") by three bridges—from north to south, the [Bayonne Bridge](/wiki/Bayonne_Bridge "Bayonne Bridge"), the [Goethals Bridge](/wiki/Goethals_Bridge "Goethals Bridge"), and the [Outerbridge Crossing](/wiki/Outerbridge_Crossing "Outerbridge Crossing").

New Jersey has [interstate compacts](/wiki/Interstate_compact "Interstate compact") with all three of its neighboring states. The [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey"), the [Delaware River Port Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_Port_Authority "Delaware River Port Authority") (with [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")), the [Delaware River Joint Toll Bridge Commission](/wiki/Delaware_River_Joint_Toll_Bridge_Commission "Delaware River Joint Toll Bridge Commission") (with Pennsylvania), and the [Delaware River and Bay Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_and_Bay_Authority "Delaware River and Bay Authority") (with [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware")) operate most of the major transportation routes in and out of the state. Bridge tolls are collected only from traffic exiting the state, with the exception of the private [Dingman's Ferry Bridge](/wiki/Dingman%27s_Ferry_Bridge "Dingman's Ferry Bridge") over the Delaware River, which charges a toll in both directions.

It is unlawful for a customer to serve themselves gasoline in New Jersey. It became the last remaining U.S. state where all [gas stations](/wiki/Filling_station "Filling station") are required to sell full-service gasoline to customers at all times in 2016, after [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon")'s introduction of restricted self-service gasoline availability took effect.[[255]](#cite_note-257)

### Airports

[Newark Liberty International Airport](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport "Newark Liberty International Airport") (EWR) is one of the [busiest airports](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_United_States "List of the busiest airports in the United States") in the United States. Operated by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey"), it is one of the three main airports serving the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), along with [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and [LaGuardia Airport](/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport"), which are both in Queens, New York. [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines") is the airport's largest tenant, operating an entire terminal (Terminal C) there, which it uses as one of its primary [hubs](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub"). [FedEx Express](/wiki/FedEx_Express "FedEx Express") operates a large [cargo terminal](/wiki/Cargo_terminal "Cargo terminal") at EWR as well. The adjacent [Newark Airport railroad station](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport_(NJT_station) "Newark Liberty International Airport (NJT station)") provides access to [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") and [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") trains along the [Northeast Corridor Line](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor_Line "Northeast Corridor Line").

Two smaller commercial airports, [Atlantic City International Airport](/wiki/Atlantic_City_International_Airport "Atlantic City International Airport") and rapidly growing [Trenton-Mercer Airport](/wiki/Trenton-Mercer_Airport "Trenton-Mercer Airport"), also operate in other parts of the state. [Teterboro Airport](/wiki/Teterboro_Airport "Teterboro Airport") in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") and [Millville Municipal Airport](/wiki/Millville_Municipal_Airport "Millville Municipal Airport") in [Cumberland County](/wiki/Cumberland_County,_New_Jersey "Cumberland County, New Jersey") are [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airports popular with [private](/wiki/Private_aviation "Private aviation") and [corporate aircraft](/wiki/Corporate_aviation "Corporate aviation") due to their proximity to New York City and the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"), respectively.

### Rail and bus

Main article: [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit")

Further information: [New Jersey Transit Bus Operations](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_Bus_Operations "New Jersey Transit Bus Operations"), [New Jersey Transit Rail Operations](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_Rail_Operations "New Jersey Transit Rail Operations"), [Port Authority Trans-Hudson](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson"), [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline"), and [List of New Jersey railroads](/wiki/List_of_New_Jersey_railroads "List of New Jersey railroads")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Hudson_bergen_exchange_place.jpg/250px-Hudson_bergen_exchange_place.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hudson_bergen_exchange_place.jpg)

Two [Hudson–Bergen Light Rail](/wiki/Hudson%E2%80%93Bergen_Light_Rail "Hudson–Bergen Light Rail") trains in [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/NEC_train_3967_passing_through_Rahway_station%2C_June_2007.jpg/250px-NEC_train_3967_passing_through_Rahway_station%2C_June_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:NEC_train_3967_passing_through_Rahway_station,_June_2007.jpg)

A [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") train on the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor") in [Rahway](/wiki/Rahway,_New_Jersey "Rahway, New Jersey")

[NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") operates extensive rail and bus service throughout the state. A state-run corporation, it began with the consolidation of several private bus companies in [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") in 1979. In the early 1980s, it acquired [Conrail](/wiki/Conrail "Conrail")'s commuter train operations that connected suburban towns to New York City. NJ Transit has [12 rail lines](/wiki/NJ_Transit_Rail_Operations "NJ Transit Rail Operations") that run through different parts of the state and 165 stations statewide.[[256]](#cite_note-258) Most of the lines end at either [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") or [Hoboken Terminal](/wiki/Hoboken_Terminal "Hoboken Terminal") in [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), although some lines provide service to both terminal stations. The [Atlantic City Line](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Line "Atlantic City Line") provides service between [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey") and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[257]](#cite_note-259)

NJ Transit also operates three [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") systems in the state. The [Hudson-Bergen Light Rail](/wiki/Hudson-Bergen_Light_Rail "Hudson-Bergen Light Rail") connects [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey") to [North Bergen](/wiki/North_Bergen,_New_Jersey "North Bergen, New Jersey"), through [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey") and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey").[[258]](#cite_note-260) The [Newark Light Rail](/wiki/Newark_Light_Rail "Newark Light Rail") is partially underground, and connects downtown [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") with other parts of the city and its suburbs, [Belleville](/wiki/Belleville,_New_Jersey "Belleville, New Jersey") and [Bloomfield](/wiki/Bloomfield,_New_Jersey "Bloomfield, New Jersey"). The [River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(New_Jersey_Transit) "River Line (New Jersey Transit)") connects [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey") and [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey").

The [PATH](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson") is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system consisting of four lines operated by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey"). It links [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), [Harrison](/wiki/Harrison,_New_Jersey "Harrison, New Jersey"), and [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") with New York City. The [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") is a rapid transit system that links [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey") to Philadelphia. Both the PATCO and the PATH are two of only five rapid transit systems in the United States to operate 24 hours a day.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") operates numerous long-distance passenger trains in New Jersey, both to and from neighboring states and around the country. In addition to the Newark Airport connection, other major Amtrak railway stations include [Trenton Transit Center](/wiki/Trenton_(Amtrak_station) "Trenton (Amtrak station)"), [Metropark](/wiki/Metropark_(NJT_station) "Metropark (NJT station)"), and the historic [Newark Penn Station](/wiki/Newark_Penn_Station "Newark Penn Station").

The [Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority](/wiki/SEPTA "SEPTA"), or SEPTA, has two commuter rail lines that operate into New Jersey. The [Trenton Line](/wiki/Trenton_Line "Trenton Line") terminates at the [Trenton Transit Center](/wiki/Trenton_Transit_Center "Trenton Transit Center"), and the [West Trenton Line](/wiki/West_Trenton_Line_(SEPTA) "West Trenton Line (SEPTA)") terminates at the [West Trenton Rail Station](/wiki/West_Trenton_Rail_Station "West Trenton Rail Station") in [Ewing](/wiki/Ewing,_New_Jersey "Ewing, New Jersey").

[AirTrain Newark](/wiki/AirTrain_Newark "AirTrain Newark") is a [monorail](/wiki/Monorail "Monorail") connecting the Amtrak/NJ Transit station on the Northeast Corridor to the airport's terminals and parking lots.

Some private bus carriers still remain in New Jersey. Most of these carriers operate with state funding to offset losses and state owned buses are provided to these carriers, of which [Coach USA](/wiki/Coach_USA "Coach USA") companies make up the bulk. Other carriers include private charter and tour bus operators that take gamblers from other parts of New Jersey, New York City, Philadelphia, and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") to the casino resorts of Atlantic City.

### Ferries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/MVCapeHenlopen.jpg/250px-MVCapeHenlopen.jpg)](/wiki/File:MVCapeHenlopen.jpg)

[Cape May–Lewes Ferry](/wiki/Cape_May%E2%80%93Lewes_Ferry "Cape May–Lewes Ferry") connects New Jersey and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") across [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay").

[New York Waterway](/wiki/NY_Waterway "NY Waterway") has ferry terminals at [Belford](/wiki/Belford,_New_Jersey "Belford, New Jersey"), [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City"), Hoboken, [Weehawken](/wiki/Weehawken "Weehawken"), and [Edgewater](/wiki/Edgewater,_New_Jersey "Edgewater, New Jersey"), with service to different parts of Manhattan. [Liberty Water Taxi](/wiki/Liberty_Water_Taxi "Liberty Water Taxi") in [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City") has ferries from [Paulus Hook](/wiki/Paulus_Hook "Paulus Hook") and [Liberty State Park](/wiki/Liberty_State_Park "Liberty State Park") to [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City") in Manhattan. [Statue Cruises](/wiki/Statue_Cruises "Statue Cruises") offers service from Liberty State Park to the [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument"), including [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island"). [SeaStreak](/wiki/SeaStreak "SeaStreak") offers services from the [Raritan Bayshore](/wiki/Raritan_Bayshore "Raritan Bayshore") to Manhattan, [Martha's Vineyard](/wiki/Martha%27s_Vineyard "Martha's Vineyard"), and [Nantucket](/wiki/Nantucket "Nantucket").

The [Delaware River and Bay Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_and_Bay_Authority "Delaware River and Bay Authority") operates the [Cape May–Lewes Ferry](/wiki/Cape_May%E2%80%93Lewes_Ferry "Cape May–Lewes Ferry") on [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"), carrying both passengers and vehicles between New Jersey and Delaware as part of [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9 "U.S. Route 9"). The agency also operates the [Forts Ferry Crossing](/wiki/Forts_Ferry_Crossing "Forts Ferry Crossing") for passengers across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"). The [Delaware River Port Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_Port_Authority "Delaware River Port Authority") operates the [RiverLink Ferry](/wiki/RiverLink_Ferry "RiverLink Ferry") between the [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") waterfront and [Penn's Landing](/wiki/Penn%27s_Landing "Penn's Landing") in Philadelphia.

## Culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/GatewayVueNB.jpg/250px-GatewayVueNB.jpg)](/wiki/File:GatewayVueNB.jpg)

[New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey"), nicknamed the *Hub city* and the *Healthcare City*, is a focus city for academia, [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare"), and culture in New Jersey, and has many [high-rise](/wiki/Tower_block "Tower block") buildings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/A_50%27s_Style_Diner.jpg/250px-A_50%27s_Style_Diner.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_50%27s_Style_Diner.jpg)

A 1950s-style [diner](/wiki/Diner "Diner") in [Orange](/wiki/Orange,_New_Jersey "Orange, New Jersey")

### General

New Jersey has continued to play a prominent role as a U.S. cultural nexus. The state has its own [cuisines](/wiki/Cuisine_of_New_Jersey "Cuisine of New Jersey"), religious communities, [museums](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_New_Jersey "List of museums in New Jersey"), and [halls of fame](/wiki/Category:Halls_of_fame_in_New_Jersey "Category:Halls of fame in New Jersey").

New Jersey is the birthplace of many modern inventions, including [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio"), the motion picture camera, the [lithium battery](/wiki/Lithium_battery "Lithium battery"), the [light bulb](/wiki/Light_bulb "Light bulb"), [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor"), and the electric train. Other New Jersey creations include: the drive-in movie, the cultivated [blueberry](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry"), [cranberry sauce](/wiki/Cranberry_sauce "Cranberry sauce"), the [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk "Boardwalk"), the [zipper](/wiki/Zipper "Zipper"), the [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph"), [saltwater taffy](/wiki/Saltwater_taffy "Saltwater taffy"), the [dirigible](/wiki/Dirigible "Dirigible"), the [seedless watermelon](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon"),[[259]](#cite_note-261) the [first use](/wiki/David_Bushnell_(inventor) "David Bushnell (inventor)") of a [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") in warfare, and the [ice cream cone](/wiki/Ice_cream_cone "Ice cream cone").[[260]](#cite_note-262)

[Diners](/wiki/Diner "Diner") are iconic to New Jersey. The state is home to many diner manufacturers and has over 600 diners, more than any other place in the world.[[261]](#cite_note-263)

New Jersey is the only state to have never had a [state song](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_songs "List of U.S. state songs");[[262]](#cite_note-264) as of 2021, it is one of only two states (the other being [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland")[[263]](#cite_note-265)) that are currently without a state song. "I'm From New Jersey" is incorrectly listed on many websites as being the New Jersey state song, but it was not even a contender when the New Jersey Arts Council submitted state song suggestions to the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") in 1996.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

New Jersey is frequently the target of jokes in American culture,[[265]](#cite_note-267) especially from New York City-based television shows, such as *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*.[[266]](#cite_note-Rockland-268) Academic [Michael Aaron Rockland](/wiki/Michael_Aaron_Rockland "Michael Aaron Rockland") attributes this to New Yorkers' view that New Jersey is the beginning of [Middle America](/wiki/Middle_America_(United_States) "Middle America (United States)"). The [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike"), which runs between two major East Coast cities, New York City and Philadelphia, is also cited as a reason, as people who traverse through the state may only see its industrial zones.[[267]](#cite_note-sjt-269) Reality television shows like *[Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore_(TV_series) "Jersey Shore (TV series)")* and *[The Real Housewives of New Jersey](/wiki/The_Real_Housewives_of_New_Jersey "The Real Housewives of New Jersey")* have reinforced stereotypical views of New Jersey culture,[[268]](#cite_note-270) but Rockland cited *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")* and the music of [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") as exporting a more positive image.[[267]](#cite_note-sjt-269)

The "New" in "New Jersey" is often omitted in casual conversation.[[269]](#cite_note-271)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Cuisine of New Jersey](/wiki/Cuisine_of_New_Jersey "Cuisine of New Jersey")

New Jersey is known for several foods developed within the region, including [Taylor Ham](/wiki/Taylor_Pork_Roll "Taylor Pork Roll") (also known as [pork roll](/wiki/Pork_roll "Pork roll")), [sloppy joe sandwiches](/wiki/Sloppy_joe_(New_Jersey) "Sloppy joe (New Jersey)"), [tomato pies](/wiki/Trenton_tomato_pie "Trenton tomato pie"), [salt water taffy](/wiki/Taffy_(candy) "Taffy (candy)"), and [Texas wieners](/wiki/Chili_dog "Chili dog"). Just as [New York City's cuisine](/wiki/Cuisine_of_New_York_City "Cuisine of New York City") has an influence on [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey"), [Philadelphia's cuisine](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Philadelphia "Cuisine of Philadelphia") influences [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey").

New Jersey's third-largest industry is food and agriculture just behind pharmaceuticals and tourism. New Jersey is one of the top 10 producers of [blueberries](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry"), [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry"), [peaches](/wiki/Peach "Peach"), [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [bell peppers](/wiki/Bell_pepper "Bell pepper"), [eggplant](/wiki/Eggplant "Eggplant"), [cucumbers](/wiki/Cucumber "Cucumber"), [apples](/wiki/Apple "Apple"), [spinach](/wiki/Spinach "Spinach"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(plant) "Squash (plant)"), and [asparagus](/wiki/Asparagus "Asparagus") in the United States. Many restaurants in the state offer locally grown ingredients because of this.[[270]](#cite_note-272)

[Campbell's Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell%27s_Soup_Company "Campbell's Soup Company") has been headquartered in [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") since 1869.[[271]](#cite_note-campbellsoupcompany.com-273) [Goya Foods](/wiki/Goya_Foods "Goya Foods"), the largest [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic")-owned food company in the United States, operates a corporate headquarters in [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey").[[272]](#cite_note-274) [Mars Wrigley Confectionery](/wiki/Wrigley_Company "Wrigley Company")'s US headquarters has been based in [Hackettstown](/wiki/Hackettstown,_New_Jersey "Hackettstown, New Jersey") and [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") since 2007.[[273]](#cite_note-275)

Several states with substantial Italian American populations take credit for the development of [submarine sandwiches](/wiki/Submarine_sandwich "Submarine sandwich"), including New Jersey.[[274]](#cite_note-276)

### Music

Main article: [Music of New Jersey](/wiki/Music_of_New_Jersey "Music of New Jersey")

New Jersey has long been an important origin for both [rock](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") and [rap](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") music. Prominent musicians from or with significant connections to New Jersey include:

* Singer [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") was born in [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"). He sang with a neighborhood vocal group, the Hoboken Four, and appeared in neighborhood theater amateur shows before he became an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning actor.
* Singer [Whitney Houston](/wiki/Whitney_Houston "Whitney Houston") was born in [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). Houston began singing at [New Hope Baptist Church](/wiki/New_Hope_Baptist_Church_(Newark) "New Hope Baptist Church (Newark)") in Newark as a child and became a background vocalist while in high school. She then became the [best-selling New Jersey musician ever](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists"), having sold over 220 million records worldwide.
* [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), who has sung of New Jersey life on most of his albums, is from [Freehold](/wiki/Freehold_Borough,_New_Jersey "Freehold Borough, New Jersey"). Some of his songs that represent New Jersey life are "[Born to Run](/wiki/Born_to_Run_(song) "Born to Run (song)")", "Spirit in the Night", "[Rosalita (Come Out Tonight)](/wiki/Rosalita_(Come_Out_Tonight) "Rosalita (Come Out Tonight)")", "[Thunder Road](/wiki/Thunder_Road_(song) "Thunder Road (song)")", "[Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City_(song) "Atlantic City (song)")", and "[Jungleland](/wiki/Jungleland "Jungleland")".
* [Irvington](/wiki/Irvington,_New_Jersey "Irvington, New Jersey")'s [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah") was one of the first female rappers to succeed in music, film, and television.[[275]](#cite_note-277)
* [Southside Johnny](/wiki/Southside_Johnny "Southside Johnny"), eponymous leader of Southside Johnny and the Asbury Jukes was raised in [Ocean Grove](/wiki/Ocean_Grove,_NJ "Ocean Grove, NJ"). He is considered the "Grandfather of the New Jersey Sound"[[276]](#cite_note-278) and is cited by Jersey-born [Jon Bon Jovi](/wiki/Jon_Bon_Jovi "Jon Bon Jovi") as his reason for singing.[[277]](#cite_note-279)
* [Jon Bon Jovi](/wiki/Jon_Bon_Jovi "Jon Bon Jovi"), from [Sayreville](/wiki/Sayreville,_New_Jersey "Sayreville, New Jersey"), reached fame in the 1980s with hard rock outfit [Bon Jovi](/wiki/Bon_Jovi "Bon Jovi"). The band has also written many songs about life in New Jersey, including "Livin' On A Prayer",[[278]](#cite_note-280) and named [one of their albums](/wiki/New_Jersey_(album) "New Jersey (album)") after the state.
* Grammy-winning jazz vocalist [Sarah Vaughan](/wiki/Sarah_Vaughan "Sarah Vaughan") was born and raised in [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). After singing in her church's choir as a child, she was sneaking into Newark nightclubs to sing and play piano by the time she was a teenager.[[279]](#cite_note-281)
* In 1964, [the Isley Brothers](/wiki/The_Isley_Brothers "The Isley Brothers") founded the record label [T-Neck Records](/wiki/T-Neck_Records "T-Neck Records"), named after [Teaneck](/wiki/Teaneck,_New_Jersey "Teaneck, New Jersey"), their home at the time.[[280]](#cite_note-282)
* The Broadway musical *[Jersey Boys](/wiki/Jersey_Boys "Jersey Boys")* is based on the lives of the members of the [Four Seasons](/wiki/The_Four_Seasons_(group) "The Four Seasons (group)"), three of whose members were born in New Jersey ([Tommy DeVito](/wiki/Tommy_DeVito_(musician) "Tommy DeVito (musician)"), [Frankie Valli](/wiki/Frankie_Valli "Frankie Valli"), and [Nick Massi](/wiki/Nick_Massi "Nick Massi")) while a fourth, [Bob Gaudio](/wiki/Bob_Gaudio "Bob Gaudio"), was born in [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") but raised in [Bergenfield](/wiki/Bergenfield,_New_Jersey "Bergenfield, New Jersey").[[281]](#cite_note-283)

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in New Jersey](/wiki/Sports_in_New_Jersey "Sports in New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg/250px-New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg)

[MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), one of only two [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") stadiums shared by two teams, is home to the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets").

New Jersey currently has six teams from [major professional sports leagues](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") playing in the state with only one team identifying themselves as solely being from New Jersey. The remaining five teams identify themselves as being from the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") with the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") team having a team name that represents both New Jersey and New York. The National Hockey League and [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") teams representing [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") have their training facilities in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey").

### Professional sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Pudcentnewarkjeh.JPG/250px-Pudcentnewarkjeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pudcentnewarkjeh.JPG)

[Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center") in [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), home of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils")

The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils"), based in [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") at the [Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center"), is the only major league [sports franchise](/wiki/Sports_franchise "Sports franchise") to bear the state's name. Founded in 1974 in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), as the [Kansas City Scouts](/wiki/Kansas_City_Scouts "Kansas City Scouts"), the team played in [Denver, Colorado](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado"), as the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies_(NHL) "Colorado Rockies (NHL)") from 1976 until the spring of 1982 when naval architect, businessman, and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City") native [John J. McMullen](/wiki/John_J._McMullen "John J. McMullen") purchased, renamed, and moved the franchise to [Brendan Byrne Arena](/wiki/Brendan_Byrne_Arena "Brendan Byrne Arena") in [East Rutherford's](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey") [Meadowlands Sports Complex](/wiki/Meadowlands_Sports_Complex "Meadowlands Sports Complex"). While the team was poor to mediocre in Kansas City, Denver, and its first years in New Jersey, qualifying for the playoffs once in the 13 seasons from 1974 to 1987, the Devils ultimately established themselves in late 1980s and early 1990s during the tenure of Hall of Fame president and general manager [Lou Lamoriello](/wiki/Lou_Lamoriello "Lou Lamoriello"). As of 2023, the Devils have appeared in 23 postseasons in 40 seasons in New Jersey, reaching five [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") Finals (most recently in [2012](/wiki/2012_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2012 Stanley Cup playoffs")) and winning it in [1995](/wiki/1995_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "1995 Stanley Cup playoffs"), [2000](/wiki/2000_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2000 Stanley Cup playoffs"), and [2003](/wiki/2003_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2003 Stanley Cup playoffs"). The organization is the youngest of the nine "Big Four" major league teams based in New York metropolitan area, ultimately establishing its core following throughout the northern and central portions of the state and carving a place in a media market once dominated by the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") and [Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") which has the distinction of being the only metropolitan area in the country with three major league professional sports teams participating in the same sport.

In 2018, the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") renovated and expanded their training facility, the Virtua Center Flyers Skate Zone, in [Voorhees Township](/wiki/Voorhees_Township,_New_Jersey "Voorhees Township, New Jersey") in the southern portion of the state.[[282]](#cite_note-284)

In 2024, the [New York Sirens](/wiki/New_York_Sirens "New York Sirens") of the [Professional Women's Hockey League](/wiki/Professional_Women%27s_Hockey_League "Professional Women's Hockey League") (PWHL) made the Prudential Center their home rink and moved their practice facility to the [Richard J. Codey Arena](/wiki/Richard_J._Codey_Arena "Richard J. Codey Arena") in [West Orange](/wiki/West_Orange,_New_Jersey "West Orange, New Jersey"), the former Devil's practice facility from 1986 to 2007.[[283]](#cite_note-285)

The [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area")'s two [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams, the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), play at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford's](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey") [Meadowlands Sports Complex](/wiki/Meadowlands_Sports_Complex "Meadowlands Sports Complex").[[284]](#cite_note-newmeadowlandsstadium.com-286) Built for about $1.6 billion,[[285]](#cite_note-Belson-287) the venue is one of the most expensive stadiums ever built.[[286]](#cite_note-Esteban-288) On February 2, 2014, MetLife Stadium hosted [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII").

The [New York Red Bulls](/wiki/New_York_Red_Bulls "New York Red Bulls") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") play in [Sports Illustrated Stadium](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated_Stadium "Sports Illustrated Stadium"), a soccer-specific stadium in [Harrison](/wiki/Harrison,_New_Jersey "Harrison, New Jersey") across the [Passaic River](/wiki/Passaic_River "Passaic River") from downtown [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). On July 27, 2011, Red Bull Arena hosted the [2011 MLS All-Star Game](/wiki/2011_MLS_All-Star_Game "2011 MLS All-Star Game").[[287]](#cite_note-289) [Gotham FC](/wiki/Gotham_FC "Gotham FC") of the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") (NWSL), founded in 2008 as Sky Blue FC, moved it's home field from [Yurcak Field](/wiki/Yurcak_Field "Yurcak Field") at [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") to Sports Illustrated Stadium in 2020.[[288]](#cite_note-290)

New Jersey hosted matches during the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") at [Giants Stadium](/wiki/Giants_Stadium "Giants Stadium") and will be one of 16 cities to host matches during the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"), at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), which will also host the [tournament final](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2026 FIFA World Cup final").[[289]](#cite_note-291)

From 1977 to 2012, New Jersey had a [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") team, the [New Jersey Nets](/wiki/New_Jersey_Nets "New Jersey Nets").[[290]](#cite_note-292) [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA")'s [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") played in New Jersey from 2011 to 2013 while their primary home arena, [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") was undergoing renovations.[[291]](#cite_note-293) In 2016, the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") of the NBA opened their new headquarters and training facility, the Philadelphia 76ers Training Complex, in [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey").[[292]](#cite_note-294)

The Meadowlands Sports Complex is home to the [Meadowlands Racetrack](/wiki/Meadowlands_Racetrack "Meadowlands Racetrack"), the major [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing") track in the state. [Monmouth Park Racetrack](/wiki/Monmouth_Park_Racetrack "Monmouth Park Racetrack") in Oceanport is a popular spot for [thoroughbred racing](/wiki/Thoroughbred_racing "Thoroughbred racing") in New Jersey and the northeast. It hosted the [Breeders' Cup](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup "Breeders' Cup") in 2007, and its turf course was renovated in preparation.

### College sports

See also: [List of college athletic programs in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_college_athletic_programs_in_New_Jersey "List of college athletic programs in New Jersey")

#### Major schools

New Jerseyans' collegiate allegiances are predominantly split among the three major [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") programs in the state: the [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93New_Brunswick "Rutgers University–New Brunswick") (New Jersey's flagship state university) [Scarlet Knights](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights "Rutgers Scarlet Knights"), members of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"); the [Seton Hall University](/wiki/Seton_Hall_University "Seton Hall University") (the state's largest [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") university) [Pirates](/wiki/Seton_Hall_Pirates "Seton Hall Pirates"), members of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference"); and the [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") (the state's [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") university) [Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers").

The intense rivalry between Rutgers and Princeton athletics began with the [first intercollegiate football game](/wiki/1869_college_football_season "1869 college football season") in 1869. The schools have not met on the football field since 1980, but they continue to play each other annually in all other sports offered by the two universities.

Rutgers, which fields 24 teams in various sports, is nationally known for its football program, with a 6–4 all-time [bowl](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game") record; and its women's basketball programs, which appeared in a [National Final](/wiki/2007_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_tournament "2007 NCAA Division I women's basketball tournament") in 2007. In 2008 and 2009, Rutgers expanded their football home, [Rutgers Stadium](/wiki/SHI_Stadium "SHI Stadium"), now called SHI Stadium, on the [Busch Campus](/wiki/Busch_Campus_of_Rutgers_University "Busch Campus of Rutgers University"). The basketball teams play at the [Rutgers Athletic Center](/wiki/Rutgers_Athletic_Center "Rutgers Athletic Center") on [Livingston Campus](/wiki/Livingston_Campus_(Rutgers_University) "Livingston Campus (Rutgers University)"). Both venues and campuses are in [Piscataway](/wiki/Piscataway_Township,_New_Jersey "Piscataway Township, New Jersey"), across the [Raritan River](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River") from [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey"). The university also fields men's basketball and baseball programs. Rutgers' fans live mostly in the western parts of the state and [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"); its alumni base is the largest in the state.

Rutgers' satellite campuses in Camden and Newark each field their own athletic programs—the [Rutgers–Camden Scarlet Raptors](/wiki/Rutgers%E2%80%93Camden_Scarlet_Raptors "Rutgers–Camden Scarlet Raptors") and the [Rutgers–Newark Scarlet Raiders](/wiki/Rutgers%E2%80%93Newark_Scarlet_Raiders "Rutgers–Newark Scarlet Raiders")—which both compete in NCAA [Division III](/wiki/Division_III_(NCAA) "Division III (NCAA)").

Seton Hall fields no football team, but its men's basketball team is one of the [Big East](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference")'s storied programs. No New Jersey team has won more games in the [NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), and it is the state's only men's basketball program to reach a modern [National Final](/wiki/1989_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1989 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"). The Pirates play their home games at [Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center") in downtown [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), about 4 miles (6 km) from the university's [South Orange](/wiki/South_Orange,_New_Jersey "South Orange, New Jersey") campus. Their fans hail largely from the predominantly Roman Catholic areas of the northern part of the state and the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore"). The annual inter-conference rivalry game between Seton Hall and Rutgers, whose venue alternates between Newark and Piscataway, the Garden State Hardwood Classic, is planned through 2026.[[293]](#cite_note-295)

#### Other schools

The state's other Division I schools include the [Monmouth University Hawks](/wiki/Monmouth_Hawks "Monmouth Hawks") (West Long Branch), the [New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT) Highlanders](/wiki/NJIT_Mens_Soccer "NJIT Mens Soccer") (Newark), the [Rider University Broncs](/wiki/Rider_University#Athletics "Rider University") (Lawrenceville), and the [Saint Peter's University Peacocks](/wiki/Saint_Peter%27s_College,_New_Jersey#Athletics "Saint Peter's College, New Jersey") (Jersey City).

[Fairleigh Dickinson University](/wiki/Fairleigh_Dickinson_Knights "Fairleigh Dickinson Knights") competes in both Division I and Division III. It has two campuses, each with its own sports teams. The teams at the Metropolitan Campus are known as the FDU Knights, and compete in the [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") and NCAA Division I. The college at Florham (FDU-Florham) teams are known as the FDU-Florham Devils and compete in the [Middle Atlantic Conferences' Freedom Conference](/wiki/Middle_Atlantic_Conferences#Freedom_Conference "Middle Atlantic Conferences") and NCAA Division III.

Among the various Division III schools in the state, the [Stevens Institute of Technology](/wiki/Stevens_Tech_Ducks_football "Stevens Tech Ducks football") Ducks have fielded the longest continuously running collegiate men's lacrosse program in the country. 2009 marked the 125th season.
