## Government and politics

Main article: [Government of New Jersey](/wiki/Government_of_New_Jersey "Government of New Jersey")

### Executive

Further information: [Governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") and [Lieutenant Governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_Jersey "Lieutenant Governor of New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Gov._Mikie_Sherril_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Gov._Mikie_Sherril_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gov._Mikie_Sherril_(cropped).jpg)

[Mikie Sherrill](/wiki/Mikie_Sherrill "Mikie Sherrill") [(D)](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")  
[57th](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_New_Jersey "List of Governors of New Jersey") [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey")  
since January 20, 2026

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Dr._Caldwell1.jpg/250px-Dr._Caldwell1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr._Caldwell1.jpg)

[Dale Caldwell](/wiki/Dale_Caldwell "Dale Caldwell") (D)  
[4th](/wiki/List_of_Lieutenant_Governors_of_New_Jersey "List of Lieutenant Governors of New Jersey") [Lt. Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_Jersey "Lieutenant Governor of New Jersey")  
since January 20, 2026

The position of Governor of New Jersey is one of the most powerful in the nation. The governor is elected on a ticket with their lieutenant governor as the only statewide elected executive officials in the state; the governor appoints the entire executive cabinet and judges of the Supreme and Superior Courts. Mikie Sherrill (D) is the governor. The governor's mansion is [Drumthwacket](/wiki/Drumthwacket "Drumthwacket"), located in [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey").

Before 2010, New Jersey was one of the few states without a [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(United_States) "Lieutenant governor (United States)"). [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [Kim Guadagno](/wiki/Kim_Guadagno "Kim Guadagno") was elected the first [lieutenant governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_Jersey "Lieutenant Governor of New Jersey") on the Republican ticket with Governor [Chris Christie](/wiki/Chris_Christie "Chris Christie") and took office on January 19, 2010. The position was created as the result of a [Constitutional amendment](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment") to the [New Jersey State Constitution](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Constitution "New Jersey State Constitution") passed by the voters in 2005. Previously a gubernatorial vacancy would be filled by the president of the [New Jersey State Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Senate "New Jersey State Senate") as acting governor, thus directing half of the legislative and all of the executive process. The current lieutenant governor is Dale Caldwell (D).

### Legislative

Main article: [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/NJ_Capitol.JPG/250px-NJ_Capitol.JPG)](/wiki/File:NJ_Capitol.JPG)

The design of the golden-domed [New Jersey State House](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_House "New Jersey State House") in [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey") differs from most other U.S. state houses in not resembling the [U.S. Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol").

The current version of the [New Jersey State Constitution](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Constitution "New Jersey State Constitution") was adopted in 1947. It provides for a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature"), consisting of an [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") [Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") of 40 members and a [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") [General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") of 80 members. Each of the 40 legislative districts elects one state senator and two Assembly members. Assembly members are elected for a two-year term in all odd-numbered years; state senators are elected in years ending in 1, 3, and 7 and thus serve either four- or two-year terms.

New Jersey is one of only five states that elects its state officials in odd-numbered years (the others are [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia")). New Jersey holds elections for these offices every four years, in the year following each federal Presidential election year.

### Judicial

Main article: [Judiciary of New Jersey](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_Jersey "Judiciary of New Jersey")

The [New Jersey Supreme Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Supreme_Court "New Jersey Supreme Court")[[296]](#cite_note-298) consists of a chief justice and six associate justices. All are appointed by the governor with the [advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent") of a majority of the membership of the state senate. Justices serve an initial seven-year term, after which they can be reappointed to serve until age 70.

Most of the day-to-day work in the New Jersey courts is carried out in the Municipal Court, where simple traffic tickets, minor criminal offenses, and small civil matters are heard.

More serious criminal and civil cases are handled by the [Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court") for each county. All Superior Court judges are appointed by the governor with the [advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent") of a majority of the membership of the state senate. Each judge serves an initial seven-year term and can be reappointed to serve until age 70. New Jersey's judiciary is unusual in that it still has separate courts of law and [equity](/wiki/Equity_(law) "Equity (law)"), like its neighbor [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") but unlike most other U.S. states. The [New Jersey Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court") is divided into Law and Chancery Divisions at the trial level; the Law Division hears both criminal cases and civil lawsuits where the plaintiff's primary remedy is [damages](/wiki/Damages "Damages"), while the Chancery Division hears family cases, civil suits where the plaintiff's primary remedy is equitable relief, and probate trials.

The Superior Court also has an [Appellate Division](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court,_Appellate_Division "New Jersey Superior Court, Appellate Division"), which functions as the state's intermediate [appellate court](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court"). Superior Court judges are assigned to the Appellate Division by the Chief Justice.

There is also a Tax Court, which is a court of limited jurisdiction. Tax Court judges hear appeals of tax decisions made by County Boards of Taxation. They also hear appeals on decisions made by the director of the Division of Taxation on such matters as state income, sales and business taxes, and homestead rebates. Appeals from Tax Court decisions are heard in the Appellate Division of Superior Court. Tax Court judges are appointed by the governor for initial terms of seven years, and upon reappointment are granted tenure until they reach the mandatory retirement age of 70. There are 12 Tax Court judgeships.

### Counties

Further information: [List of New Jersey counties](/wiki/List_of_New_Jersey_counties "List of New Jersey counties")

New Jersey is divided into 21 counties; 13 date from the colonial era. New Jersey was completely divided into counties by 1692; the present counties were created by dividing the existing ones; most recently [Union County](/wiki/Union_County,_New_Jersey "Union County, New Jersey") in 1857.[[297]](#cite_note-299) New Jersey was formerly the only state in the nation where elected county officials were called freeholders. Elected county officials are now called county commissioners as of bill S855 signed by Governor Murphy on August 8, 2020. The county commissioners govern each county as part of its own [Board of Chosen County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)")[[298]](#cite_note-300) The number of county commissioners in each county is determined by referendum, and must consist of three, five, seven or nine members.

Depending on the county, the executive and [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") functions may be performed by the [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)") or split into separate branches of government. In 16 counties, the County Commissioners perform both legislative and executive functions on a commission basis, with each commissioner assigned responsibility for a department or group of departments. In the other five counties ([Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "Atlantic County, New Jersey"), [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex_County,_New_Jersey "Essex County, New Jersey"), [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey") and [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey")), there is a directly elected [County Executive](/wiki/County_Executive "County Executive") who performs the executive functions while the commissioners retain a legislative and oversight role. In counties without an Executive, a County Administrator (or County Manager) may be hired to perform day-to-day administration of county functions.[[299]](#cite_note-301)

### Municipalities

Further information: [List of municipalities in New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_New_Jersey "List of municipalities in New Jersey")

New Jersey currently has 564 municipalities; the most recent dissolution of a municipality was when [Pine Valley](/wiki/Pine_Valley,_New_Jersey "Pine Valley, New Jersey") merged into [Pine Hill](/wiki/Pine_Hill,_New_Jersey "Pine Hill, New Jersey") on January 1, 2022. Unlike other states, all New Jersey land is part of a municipality. In 2008, Governor [Jon Corzine](/wiki/Jon_Corzine "Jon Corzine") proposed cutting state aid to all towns under 10,000 people, to encourage mergers to reduce administrative costs.[[300]](#cite_note-302) In May 2009, the Local Unit Alignment Reorganization and Consolidation Commission began a study of about 40 small communities in South Jersey to decide which ones might be good candidates for consolidation.[[301]](#cite_note-303)

#### Forms of municipal government

| [New Jersey municipal government](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey "Local government in New Jersey") |

| Modern forms |
| * [Walsh Act commission](/wiki/Walsh_Act "Walsh Act") * [1923 municipal manager](/wiki/1923_Municipal_Manager_Law "1923 Municipal Manager Law") |
| [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act") forms |
| * [Mayor–council](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(mayor%E2%80%93council) "Faulkner Act (mayor–council)") * [Council–manager](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(council%E2%80%93manager) "Faulkner Act (council–manager)") * [Small municipality](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(small_municipality) "Faulkner Act (small municipality)") * [Mayor–council–administrator](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(mayor%E2%80%93council%E2%80%93administrator) "Faulkner Act (mayor–council–administrator)") |
| Nonstandard forms |

Starting in the 20th century, largely driven by reform-minded goals, a series of six modern forms of government was implemented. This began with the [Walsh Act](/wiki/Walsh_Act_(New_Jersey) "Walsh Act (New Jersey)"), enacted in 1911 by the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature"), which provided for a three- or five-member commission elected on a non-partisan basis. This was followed by the [1923 Municipal Manager Law](/wiki/1923_Municipal_Manager_Law "1923 Municipal Manager Law"), which offered a non-partisan council, provided for a weak mayor elected by and from the members of the council, and introduced a [Council-manager government](/wiki/Council-manager_government "Council-manager government") structure with an appointed manager responsible for the day-to-day administration of municipal affairs.

The [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(New_Jersey) "Faulkner Act (New Jersey)"), originally enacted in 1950 and substantially amended in 1981, offers four basic plans: [Mayor-Council](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(Mayor-Council) "Faulkner Act (Mayor-Council)"), [Council-Manager](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(Council-Manager) "Faulkner Act (Council-Manager)"), [Small Municipality](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(Small_Municipality) "Faulkner Act (Small Municipality)"), and [Mayor-Council-Administrator](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(Mayor-Council-Administrator) "Faulkner Act (Mayor-Council-Administrator)"). The act provides many choices for communities with a preference for a strong executive and professional management of municipal affairs and offers great flexibility in allowing municipalities to select the characteristics of its government: the number of seats on the council; seats selected at-large, by wards, or through a combination of both; staggered or concurrent terms of office; and a mayor chosen by the council or elected directly by voters. Most large municipalities and a majority of New Jersey's residents are governed by municipalities with Faulkner Act charters. Municipalities can also formulate their own unique form of government and operate under a [Special Charter](/wiki/Special_Charter_(New_Jersey) "Special Charter (New Jersey)") with the approval of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature").[[302]](#cite_note-304)

While municipalities retain their names derived from types of government, they may have changed to one of the modern forms of government, or further in the past to one of the other traditional forms, leading to municipalities with formal names quite baffling to the general public. For example, though there are four municipalities that are officially of the village type, none use the village form of government. [Loch Arbour](/wiki/Loch_Arbour,_New_Jersey "Loch Arbour, New Jersey") and [Ridgefield Park](/wiki/Ridgefield_Park,_New_Jersey "Ridgefield Park, New Jersey") (now with a Walsh Act form), [Ridgewood](/wiki/Ridgewood,_New_Jersey "Ridgewood, New Jersey") (now with a Faulkner Act Council-Manager charter) and [South Orange](/wiki/South_Orange,_New_Jersey "South Orange, New Jersey") (now operates under a [Special Charter](/wiki/Special_Charter_(New_Jersey) "Special Charter (New Jersey)")) all migrated to other non-village forms.[[303]](#cite_note-305)[[304]](#cite_note-306)

### Politics

Main article: [Politics of New Jersey](/wiki/Politics_of_New_Jersey "Politics of New Jersey")

#### Social attitudes and issues

See also: [LGBT rights in New Jersey](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_New_Jersey "LGBT rights in New Jersey") and [Gun laws in New Jersey](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_New_Jersey "Gun laws in New Jersey")

Socially, New Jersey is considered one of the more liberal states in the nation. Polls indicate that 60% of the population are self-described as [pro-choice](/wiki/Pro-choice "Pro-choice"), although a majority are opposed to late trimester and [intact dilation and extraction](/wiki/Intact_dilation_and_extraction "Intact dilation and extraction") and public funding of abortion.[[305]](#cite_note-307)[[306]](#cite_note-308) As of 2022, all aspects of reproductive choice (including abortion) are protected by law.[[307]](#cite_note-Repro-309)

In a 2009 [Quinnipiac University Polling Institute](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University_Polling_Institute "Quinnipiac University Polling Institute") poll, a plurality supported [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_Jersey "Same-sex marriage in New Jersey") 49% to 43% opposed.[[308]](#cite_note-310) On October 18, 2013, the [New Jersey Supreme Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Supreme_Court "New Jersey Supreme Court") rendered a provisional, unanimous (7–0) order authorizing same-sex marriage in the state, pending a legal appeal by Governor Chris Christie,[[309]](#cite_note-311) who then withdrew this appeal hours after the inaugural same-sex marriages took place on October 21, 2013.[[310]](#cite_note-DroppedAppeal-312)

New Jersey also has some of the most stringent [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control") laws in the U.S. These include bans on [assault firearms](/wiki/Assault_weapon "Assault weapon"), hollow-nose bullets and slingshots. No gun offense in New Jersey is graded less than a felony. [BB guns](/wiki/BB_guns "BB guns") and black-powder guns are all treated as modern firearms. New Jersey does not recognize out-of-state gun licenses and aggressively enforces its own gun laws.[[311]](#cite_note-313)

In 2020, the state's voting population passed a public question[[312]](#cite_note-MJ-314) that amended the state constitution to legalize [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana") and erase past legal convictions for possession. The measure passed by a two-thirds vote. At the time the measure was enacted, about a dozen other U.S. states had also legalized the sale and possession of marijuana. As of 2024, local governments and municipalities are still in the process of regulating marijuana-related businesses within their jurisdictions.

#### Elections

Main article: [Elections in New Jersey](/wiki/Elections_in_New_Jersey "Elections in New Jersey")

See also: [Political party strength in New Jersey](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_New_Jersey "Political party strength in New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Cory_Booker%2C_official_portrait%2C_114th_Congress.jpg/250px-Cory_Booker%2C_official_portrait%2C_114th_Congress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cory_Booker,_official_portrait,_114th_Congress.jpg)

[Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") [(D)](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")  
Senior [U.S. Senator](/wiki/U.S._Senator "U.S. Senator")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Congressman_Andy_Kim_%28NJ-03%29_Headshot_Photo_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Congressman_Andy_Kim_%28NJ-03%29_Headshot_Photo_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congressman_Andy_Kim_(NJ-03)_Headshot_Photo_(cropped).jpg)

[Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim_(politician) "Andy Kim (politician)") (D)  
Junior U.S. Senator

New Jersey is a [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") stronghold. [New Jersey Democrats](/wiki/New_Jersey_Democratic_State_Committee "New Jersey Democratic State Committee") have majority control of both houses of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") (Senate, 24–16, and Assembly, 46–34), 9–3 split of the state's twelve seats in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and both [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") seats. There have been recent Republican governors, however: [Christine Todd Whitman](/wiki/Christine_Todd_Whitman "Christine Todd Whitman") won election in 1993 and 1997 and [Chris Christie](/wiki/Chris_Christie "Chris Christie") in 2009 and 2013.

In federal elections, the state leans heavily towards the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), having last voted for a Republican for president in 1988. New Jersey was a crucial [swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") in the elections of [1960](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election"), [1968](/wiki/1968_United_States_presidential_election "1968 United States presidential election"), and [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election"). The last elected Republican to hold a Senate seat from New Jersey was [Clifford P. Case](/wiki/Clifford_P._Case "Clifford P. Case") in 1979. Newark Mayor [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") was elected in October 2013 to join [Robert Menendez](/wiki/Bob_Menendez "Bob Menendez") to make New Jersey the first state with concurrently serving black and Latino U.S. senators.[[313]](#cite_note-315)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/New_Jersey_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg/250px-New_Jersey_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_Jersey_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg)

[2024 U.S. presidential election results](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_Jersey "2024 United States presidential election in New Jersey") by county in New Jersey

Democratic

Republican

The state's Democratic strongholds include [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), [Essex County](/wiki/Essex_County,_New_Jersey "Essex County, New Jersey") (the state's most Democratic county—it includes [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), the state's largest city), [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey") (the second-strongest Democratic county, including [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), the state's second-largest city); [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey") (especially around [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey") and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey")), [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_New_Jersey "Middlesex County, New Jersey"), and [Union County](/wiki/Union_County,_New_Jersey "Union County, New Jersey") (including [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_New_Jersey "Elizabeth, New Jersey"), the state's fourth-largest city).[[314]](#cite_note-316) Other suburban counties, especially [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") and [Burlington County](/wiki/Burlington_County,_New_Jersey "Burlington County, New Jersey"), had the majority of votes go to the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)").

The northwestern and southeastern counties of the state are reliably Republican: Republicans have support along the coast in [Ocean County](/wiki/Ocean_County,_New_Jersey "Ocean County, New Jersey") and [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey") as well as in the mountainous northwestern part of the state, especially in [Hunterdon County](/wiki/Hunterdon_County,_New_Jersey "Hunterdon County, New Jersey"), [Sussex County](/wiki/Sussex_County,_New_Jersey "Sussex County, New Jersey"), and [Warren County](/wiki/Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Warren County, New Jersey").

To be eligible to vote in a U.S. election, all New Jerseyans are required to start their residency in the state 30 days prior to an election and register 21 days prior to election day.[[315]](#cite_note-317)

#### Capital punishment

Main article: [Capital punishment in New Jersey](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_New_Jersey "Capital punishment in New Jersey")

On December 17, 2007, Governor Jon Corzine signed into law a bill that would eliminate the death penalty in New Jersey. New Jersey was the first state to pass such legislation since [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") and [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") eliminated executions in 1965.[[316]](#cite_note-318) Corzine also signed a bill that would downgrade the Death Row prisoners' sentences from "Death" to "Life in Prison with No Parole".[[317]](#cite_note-319)

### Boardwalks

[![People walking along a wide pathway near the ocean on a sunny day](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_view_north_from_Caesars_Atlantic_City_by_Silveira_Neto_June_24_2012.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_view_north_from_Caesars_Atlantic_City_by_Silveira_Neto_June_24_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_view_north_from_Caesars_Atlantic_City_by_Silveira_Neto_June_24_2012.jpg)

The [Atlantic City Boardwalk](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk "Atlantic City Boardwalk") seen from [Caesars Atlantic City](/wiki/Caesars_Atlantic_City "Caesars Atlantic City"), which opened in 1870 as the world's first [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)")[[318]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest1-320) At 5+1⁄2 miles (8.9 km) long, it is also the world's longest and busiest boardwalk.[[319]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest2-321) New Jersey is home to the world's highest concentration of boardwalks.

Many communities along the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore") have a boardwalk with various attractions, entertainment, shopping, dining, arcades, water parks, and amusement parks. The [Atlantic City Boardwalk](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk "Atlantic City Boardwalk"), opened in 1870, as the world's first [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)").[[318]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest1-320) At 5+1⁄2 miles (8.9 km) long, it is also the world's longest and busiest boardwalk.[[224]](#cite_note-Oyster2015-226)[[319]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest2-321)

The skyline of [Six Flags Great Adventure](/wiki/Six_Flags_Great_Adventure "Six Flags Great Adventure") in [Jackson Township](/wiki/Jackson_Township,_New_Jersey "Jackson Township, New Jersey") in [Ocean County](/wiki/Ocean_County,_New_Jersey "Ocean County, New Jersey"), the world's largest [theme park](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park") as of 2013[[323]](#cite_note-325) To the far left is [Kingda Ka](/wiki/Kingda_Ka "Kingda Ka"), the world's tallest [roller coaster](/wiki/Roller_coaster "Roller coaster") until its closure in 2024.[[324]](#cite_note-326)[[325]](#cite_note-NBC_New_York_2024-327)

Retrieved March 10, 2024. "Answer time: East Brunswick has held the statistical center of population title since the 2010 Census.... 304. **[^](#cite_ref-306)** Shields, Nancy. ["Loch Arbour to change government; staying village for now; Will switch from five-member board of trustees to three-member board of commissioners"](https://www.newspapers.com/clip/98717297/loch-arbour-changes-from-village-form/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20230208010738/https://www.newspapers.com/clip/98717297/loch-arbour-changes-from-village-form/) February 8, 2023, at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), *[Asbury Park Press](/wiki/Asbury_Park_Press "Asbury Park Press")*, December 21, 2011. "Village residents Tuesday voted 51–35 to change its form of government from a five-member board of trustees to a three-member board of commissioners. The vote in the special election means that Loch Arbour is still a village, at least for now, but the town will have three commissioners elected at the same time every four years.... The special election to change the form of government under the Walsh Act is part of a number of steps being taken in search of an answer to a very high school-tax bill, which residents were forced to take on after the state in 2008 ended a special financial deal the village worked out with Ocean Township a decade ago to keep school taxes low."
